<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Secure Transmission Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alice - Sender] -->|"1. Plaintext M"| B[Compute SHA-256 hash h]
    B -->|"2. Hash h"| C[Sign with Alice Private Key]
    C -->|"3. Signature sigma"| D[Encrypt M with Bob Public Key]
    D -->|"4. Ciphertext C"| E[Transmit over Insecure Channel]
    E -->|"5. Receive C, sigma"| F[Bob - Receiver]
    F -->|"6. Decrypt with Bob Private Key"| G[Recover Plaintext M]
    G -->|"7. Recompute h prime = SHA-256 M"| H[Verify sigma using Alice Public Key]
    H -->|"Match"| I[Accept - Authentic and Integral]
    H -->|"Mismatch"| J[Reject - Tampered or Forged]
```

## 4.2 Digital Signature Lifecycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KEYGEN[Phase 1 - Key Generation]
        K1[Select primes p and q] --> K2[Compute n = p q]
        K2 --> K3[Compute phi n]
        K3 --> K4[Pick e gcd 1]
        K4 --> K5[Compute d such that e d mod phi n = 1]
        K5 --> K6P[Public Key n e]
        K5 --> K6Q[Private Key n d]
    end

    subgraph SIGN[Phase 2 - Signing]
        S1[Message M] --> S2[Hash h = SHA-256 M]
        S2 --> S3[sigma = h to the power d mod n]
        S3 --> S4[Output sigma]
    end

    subgraph VERIFY[Phase 3 - Verification]
        V1[Received M prime] --> V2[Hash h prime = SHA-256 M prime]
        V2 --> V3[h double prime = sigma to the power e mod n]
        V3 --> V4{h prime equals h double prime}
        V4 -->|Yes| V5[VALID]
        V4 -->|No| V6[INVALID]
    end

    K6Q --> S3
    K6P --> V3
```

## 4.3 Threat Model: What Each Component Defends Against

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Network Sniffing Eavesdropper] -->|Mitigated by| M1[Asymmetric Encryption RSA OAEP]
    T2[Man in the Middle Forgery] -->|Mitigated by| M2[Digital Signature RSA PKCS1 v1.5 or PSS]
    T3[Message Tampering on the Wire] -->|Mitigated by| M3[Hash Function SHA-256 Integrity]
    T4[Sender Repudiation I never sent this] -->|Mitigated by| M4[Non Repudiation via Private Key]
    T5[Replay Attack Resend old signed msg] -->|Mitigated by| M5[Timestamp or Nonce in Message]
    T6[Hash Collision Craft two msgs with same hash] -->|Mitigated by| M6[Collision Resistant SHA-256]
```

## 4.4 RSA Sign-Verify Data Flow with Bit-Level Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plaintext M arbitrary length] --> P2[Pad to block size 2048 bits]
    P2 --> P3[SHA-256 Compress 256 bits]
    P3 --> P4[PKCS1 v1.5 Encode DER with DigestInfo]
    P4 --> P5[RSA Private Operation m to the power d mod n]
    P5 --> P6[Signature 2048 bits]
    P6 --> P7[Transmit to Receiver]

    P7 --> P8[RSA Public Operation s to the power e mod n]
    P8 --> P9[Extract Hash from DigestInfo]
    P9 --> P10[Recompute SHA-256 of received M]
    P10 --> P11{Bitwise Compare}
    P11 -->|Equal| P12[Valid Signature]
    P11 -->|Different| P13[Reject Signature]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a Digital Signature. State two situations where a digital signature is preferred over a MAC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MAC | Digital Signature |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Key used | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (private key to sign, public key to verify) |
| Verification | Only parties with the shared key can verify | Anyone with the public key can verify |
| Non-repudiation | ❌ No (sender can deny, both share the key) | ✅ Yes (only sender has the private key) |
| Speed | Faster (hash-based, e.g., HMAC-SHA256) | Slower (asymmetric math) |
| Use case | Internal API authentication, JWT integrity | Legal documents, software distribution, public certificates |

**Two situations favoring digital signature:**
1. **Legal/contractual documents** — non-repudiation is required so the signer cannot later deny the action.
2. **Public software/code distribution** — thousands of users need to verify the publisher without holding a shared secret.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three essential properties of a cryptographic hash function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Pre-image resistance (One-way property):** Given a hash value $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any message $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. Prevents an attacker from recovering the original message from its digest.

2. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given a message $M_1$, it is infeasible to find a different message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. Prevents an attacker from substituting a fraudulent message with the same hash.

3. **Collision resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find any two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This is a stronger property than second pre-image resistance and is the basis of digital signature security.

*(Optional 4th property for extra credit: Avalanche effect — small change in input drastically changes the output.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. The model below follows the exact split: **part (a) 7 marks + part (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of generating and verifying a digital signature using RSA. State the mathematical equations for signing and verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sender Alice wants to send a secure message $M$ to receiver Bob such that the message is **confidential, authentic, and integral**. Design a complete protocol showing all keys, hash operations, encryption steps, and verification steps. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (reproduce in answer sheet):**
```
  ┌─────────────────┐                  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  Alice (Sender) │                  │  Bob (Receiver) │
  └────────┬────────┘                  └────────▲────────┘
           │                                    │
   ┌───────▼────────┐                   ┌──────┴───────┐
   │  Compute       │                   │  Verify      │
   │  h = SHA-256(M)│                   │  h' = σ^e mod n│
   └───────┬────────┘                   └──────┬───────┘
           │                                    │
   ┌───────▼────────┐                   ┌──────▼───────┐
   │  Sign          │  ────  σ  ──────► │  Compare     │
   │  σ = h^d mod n │                   │  h' ?= h     │
   └────────────────┘                   └──────────────┘
```

**Mathematical Equations (2 marks):**
- **Key generation:** $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$
- **Signing:** $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \mod n$
- **Verification:** $H(M) \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^{e} \mod n$

**Explanation (5 marks):**
1. **Key generation:** Alice generates two large primes $p, q$ and computes the public/private key pair.
2. **Hashing:** The message $M$ of arbitrary length is passed through SHA-256 to obtain a fixed 256-bit digest $h$.
3. **Signing:** Alice applies RSA private-key exponentiation to $h$, producing signature $\sigma$.
4. **Transmission:** The tuple $(M, \sigma)$ is sent to Bob.
5. **Verification:** Bob recomputes $h' = \sigma^e \mod n$ and compares with his own hash of $M$. If equal, the signature is valid.

### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Protocol Design (full marks break-up):**

| Step | Alice (Sender) Action | Bob (Receiver) Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Generate own RSA key pair $(e_A, d_A, n_A)$ | Generate own RSA key pair $(e_B, d_B, n_B)$ |
| 2 | Compute $h = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | — |
| 3 | Sign: $\sigma = h^{d_A} \mod n_A$ | — |
| 4 | Encrypt: $C = M^{e_B} \mod n_B$ | — |
| 5 | Send $(C, \sigma)$ over insecure channel | Receive $(C, \sigma)$ |
| 6 | — | Decrypt: $M = C^{d_B} \mod n_B$ |
| 7 | — | Recompute: $h' = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ |
| 8 | — | Verify: $h' \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^{e_A} \mod n_A$ |

- **Confidentiality:** Step 4 — only Bob can decrypt (private key $d_B$ is secret).
- **Authenticity:** Step 8 — only Alice has $d_A$, so a valid signature proves her identity.
- **Integrity:** Step 7 — any change in $M$ alters $h'$, failing verification.
- **Non-repudiation:** Since only Alice possesses $d_A$, she cannot later deny sending $M$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the SHA-256 algorithm with its internal block structure (message padding, parsing, compression function). Show the round function structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform RSA signature generation and verification for a small toy example. Choose $p=5$, $q=11$, $e=3$, message hash $H(M)=4$. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**SHA-256 Algorithm (N = 1024-bit blocks, output 256 bits):**

1. **Padding:** Append a '1' bit, then '0' bits, then a 64-bit length field so that total length ≡ 896 mod 1024.
2. **Parsing:** Break padded message into $N$ 1024-bit blocks $M^{(1)}, M^{(2)}, \ldots, M^{(N)}$.
3. **Initial Hash Value (IV):** Eight 32-bit words $H_0^{(0)} \ldots H_7^{(0)}$ derived from square roots of first 8 primes.
4. **For each block:**
   - Prepare message schedule $W_t$ (64 32-bit words, first 16 from block, rest computed)
   - Initialize working variables $a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$ from previous hash
   - **64 rounds of compression:** each round uses 6 logical functions (Ch, Maj, Σ0, Σ1, σ0, σ1) and a round constant $K_t$
   - Compute new intermediate hash
5. **Final hash:** Concatenate $H_0^{(N)} \ldots H_7^{(N)}$.

**Round Function (one of 64):**
$$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t$$
$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c)$$
$$h = g, \; g = f, \; f = e, \; e = d + T_1$$
$$d = c, \; c = b, \; b = a, \; a = T_1 + T_2$$

Where:
- $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\neg x \land z)$
- $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$

### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $e = 3$, $H(M) = 4$

**Step 1:** $n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55$
**Step 2:** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$
**Step 3:** Find $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, i.e., $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$

Test: $3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1$ ✓ → $d = 27$

**Public key:** $(n=55, e=3)$   |   **Private key:** $(n=55, d=27)$

**Step 4 (Sign):** $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \mod n = 4^{27} \mod 55$

Compute step-by-step using repeated squaring:
- $4^1 = 4$
- $4^2 = 16$
- $4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \mod 55 = 256 - 4(55) = 256 - 220 = 36$
- $4^8 = 36^2 = 1296 \mod 55$. $1296 / 55 = 23$ rem $1296 - 1265 = 31$
- $4^{16} = 31^2 = 961 \mod 55$. $961 / 55 = 17$ rem $961 - 935 = 26$
- $4^{27} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^8 \cdot 4^2 \cdot 4^1 = 26 \cdot 31 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 \mod 55$
- $26 \cdot 31 = 806 \mod 55 = 806 - 14(55) = 806 - 770 = 36$
- $36 \cdot 16 = 576 \mod 55 = 576 - 10(55) = 576 - 550 = 26$
- $26 \cdot 4 = 104 \mod 55 = 104 - 55 = 49$

**Signature:** $\sigma = 49$

**Step 5 (Verify):** Compute $\sigma^{e} \mod n = 49^3 \mod 55$
- $49^2 = 2401 \mod 55$. $2401 / 55 = 43$ rem $2401 - 2365 = 36$
- $49^3 = 36 \cdot 49 \mod 55 = 1764 \mod 55$. $1764 / 55 = 32$ rem $1764 - 1760 = 4$

**Verified hash = 4** ✅

**Conclusion:** Since verified hash ($4$) equals the original $H(M) = 4$, the signature is **VALID**. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting the hash step** — directly "signing" the message without hashing. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up $d$ and $e$** — confusing public/private exponent in equations. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **No "why" explained** — only writing equations without stating *why* SHA-256 is used (fixed-length, integrity, collision resistance). (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 4. **No tampering test in lab record** — KTU examiners specifically want to see that the signature FAILS when the message is altered. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Wrong output for SHA-256** — confusing the hex format, missing padding explanation, or omitting the 64-bit length field. (Lose 2 marks)
> 6. **Not labelling the diagram** — block diagram without keys/algorithms annotated. (Lose 1 mark)
> 7. **Forgetting non-repudiation** — the most distinctive property of digital signatures over MACs. Always mention it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **digital signature** provides **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation** — but **not confidentiality** (that needs encryption).
- ✅ The **hash function** is the heart of the integrity guarantee; **SHA-256** is the KTU-recommended algorithm. MD5 and SHA-1 are **broken/deprecated** — never recommend them in exams.
- ✅ **Signing equation** (RSA): $\sigma = H(M)^{d} \mod n$   |   **Verification:** $H(M) \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^{e} \mod n$
- ✅ **Key generation** requires: pick primes $p,q$; compute $n = pq$; $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$; pick $e$; compute $d = e^{-1} \mod \phi(n)$.
- ✅ The **avalanche effect** (~50% bit change) is a defining property of good hash functions.
- ✅ Always **sign the hash, not the message** — for performance, fixed-size input, and to leverage collision resistance.
- ✅ **RSA-PSS** is preferred over **RSA-PKCS1v1.5** for new systems (provable security).
- ✅ **ECDSA/EdDSA** use smaller keys for the same security level (256-bit ECDSA ≈ 3072-bit RSA).
- ✅ The lab experiment **must demonstrate** both **successful verification** and **tampering detection**.
- ✅ **MAC vs Digital Signature** — MAC is symmetric, faster, no non-repudiation. Digital signature is asymmetric, slower, has non-repudiation.
- ✅ **Quantum computers (Shor's algorithm)** will break RSA/ECDSA; post-quantum signature schemes (**Dilithium, Falcon, SPHINCS+**) are being standardized by NIST.
- ✅ Lab tools: **PyCryptodome** (Python), **KeyTool** (Java), **OpenSSL** (CLI) — know at least one.
- ✅ For KTU viva: be ready to explain **why** the tampering test works (hash of tampered message ≠ hash in signature → verification fails).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration and deployment of Snort as an Intrusion Detection System (IDS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Configuration and deployment of Snort as an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) # 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Snort** is an **open-source, signature-based Network Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (NIDS/NIPS)** that performs **real-time traffic analysis**, **protocol inspection**, **content searching/matching**, and **packet logging** on Internet Protocol (IP) networks. It is officially classified as a **rule-driven packet inspection engine** that operates across the OSI layers (primarily Layers 2–7) to detect malicious activities such as port scans, buffer overflow attempts, web application attacks, and policy violations. Snort was originally authored by **Martin Roesch** in 1998 and is currently maintained by **Cisco Systems** under the **GNU General Public License (GPLv2)**. It is officially recommended by the **SANS Institute** and is widely deployed in enterprise SOCs (Security Operations Centers) as a first-line defense. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme NEP Alignment:** This experiment directly maps to **Course Outcome CO5** — *"Configure and deploy standard network security tools to monitor, detect, and prevent intrusions in a simulated network environment."* It also touches **CO4** (Cryptographic and network security mechanisms) and supports the **Skill-Based Laboratory** assessment rubric under the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security Checkpoint" Model Imagine **Snort** as a **highly trained, hyper-vigilant Airport Security Officer (ASO)** stationed at the entrance of a secure terminal: | Airport Security Analogy | Snort IDS Component | |:---|:---| | 🧍 Travelers walking through the gate | Network packets traversing the wire | | 🎫 The ASO at the X-ray scanner | Snort's **Packet Decoder** | | 📋 The rulebook the ASO follows | The **snort.conf** configuration + **rules files** | | 🔍 The metal detector & X-ray machine | **Preprocessors** (normalize, reassemble, decode) | | 🧠 The ASO's brain deciding "threat or not?" | The **Detection Engine** | | 📢 The loudspeaker that announces alarms | **Output/Alerting Modules** | | 📹 The CCTV recording the entire scene | **Packet Logger** | Just as the ASO has a **checklist of suspicious behaviors** (bulky coats, unattended bags, prohibited liquids), Snort holds a **rule database** that defines what constitutes a "malicious packet." When a packet matches a rule, Snort raises an **ALERT** — exactly like the ASO calling out, *"STOP! Step aside, please!"* > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Snort doesn't *prevent* traffic by default (that's an IPS in inline mode). It *watches*, *analyzes*, and *alerts* — the network's silent digital sentinel. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology Every KTU Student Must Memorize - **IDS (Intrusion Detection System):** A passive monitoring system that detects and alerts on suspicious activity without blocking it. - **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System):** An active system that can *block* malicious traffic inline (Snort supports this via DAQ/AFPacket). - **Signature-based detection:** Pattern matching against known attack signatures (the dominant Snort mode). - **Anomaly-based detection:** Behavioral baselining (Snort has limited support; primarily signature-based). - **NIDS vs HIDS:** *Network-based* IDS (monitors wire traffic — Snort's role) vs *Host-based* IDS (monitors a single machine). - **PCAP (Packet Capture):** Standard file format (`.pcap`) for storing captured network traffic. - **DAQ (Data Acquisition Library):** Snort's abstraction layer for packet capture (e.g., `pcap`, `afpacket`, `nfq`). --- ## 1.4 Operational Modes of Snort (KTU Viva Favorite 🎤) | Mode | Command Flag | Purpose | |:---|:---|:---| | **Sniffer Mode** | `snort -v` | Displays packet headers (verbose console) | | **Packet Logger Mode** | `snort -l ./log -b` | Logs packets to disk in binary `.pcap` form | | **Network IDS Mode** | `snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0` | Full IDS — analyzes traffic against rules and generates alerts | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Viva Trap:** Examiners often ask *"What is the difference between sniffer and IDS mode?"* The crisp answer: *Sniffer mode only displays packets on screen; IDS mode applies the full detection engine and rule set, generating alerts based on rule matches.* --- # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Snort's Internal Architecture — The 4-Stage Processing Pipeline Snort operates as a **modular, pipeline-based packet processing engine**. Every packet flows through the following stages in strict sequential order: ### 🧩 Stage 1: **Packet Decoder (The Listener)** - Receives raw bytes from the **DAQ library** (which uses `libpcap` or `af_packet` under the hood). - Decodes Layer 2 (Ethernet), Layer 3 (IP/ICMP/ARP), and Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) headers. - Validates basic protocol integrity (e.g., IP checksum, TCP flags). - Forwards a structured *packet struct* to the preprocessors. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** If the decoder fails, the packet is dropped *before* any rule is even considered. This is why malformed/fuzzed packets can sometimes evade detection — they break the decoder first. ### 🧩 Stage 2: **Preprocessors (The Normalizers)** - Reassemble fragmented packets (e.g., `frag3`, `ip4`, `ip6`). - Decode application-layer protocols: HTTP (`http_inspect`), SMTP (`smtp`), DNS (`dns`), SSL/TLS (`ssl`), FTP/Telnet (`ftp_telnet`). - Detect port-scan patterns: `sfportscan`, `portscan`. - Normalize encoding tricks (URL encoding, double-encoding) to prevent evasion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The famous *fragmentation overlap* attack** is defeated by Snort's `frag3` preprocessor using a "BSD-right" or "BSD-left" policy that defines which fragment's data wins in an overlap. ### 🧩 Stage 3: **Detection Engine (The Brain)** 🧠 - The **heart of Snort**. - Applies each enabled rule (from the `*.rules` files) against the packet. - Uses optimized pattern-matching algorithms: - **Aho-Corasick** (multi-pattern string search) - **Hyperscan** (Intel's high-performance regex engine) - **Boyer-Moore** fallback - A single packet may match **multiple rules**; Snort picks the **highest-priority match** by default (configurable via the `classify` and `priority` rule keywords). ### 🧩 Stage 4: **Output / Alerting Modules (The Voice)** - Takes action when a rule fires: `alert`, `log`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop`. - Outputs to multiple sinks: `alert_fast` (stdout), `alert_full`, `alert_unixsock`, `log_tcpdump` (pcap), `unified2` (binary format for Barnyard2/Sguil), `alert_syslog`, `database` (MySQL/PostgreSQL). --- ## 2.2 Snort Rule Anatomy — The DNA of Every Detection Every Snort rule follows the **two-section, single-line grammar**: ``` ACTION PROTOCOL SRC_IP SRC_PORT -> DST_IP DST_PORT (OPTIONS) ^^ ^^ ^^ ^^ ^^ ^^ ^^ ^^ Header Proto Source Destination Rule Options ``` ### 🔬 Header Section Breakdown | Field | Allowed Values | Example | |:---|:---|:---| | **Action** | `alert`, `log`, `pass`, `activate`, `dynamic`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop` | `alert` | | **Protocol** | `tcp`, `udp`, `icmp`, `ip`, `http`, `ssl`, `dns` | `tcp` | | **Source IP** | IP, CIDR, `any`, list `$HOME_NET`, negation `!` | `any` | | **Source Port** | Port, range, `any`, list, negation | `any` | | **Direction** | `->` (uni-directional) or `<>` (bi-directional) | `->` | | **Destination IP** | Same as source | `$HOME_NET` | | **Destination Port** | Same as source port | `80` | ### 🔬 Rule Options Section (The Detection Logic) Options are enclosed in parentheses and use `key:value` pairs separated by semicolons: | Keyword | Meaning | Example | |:---|:---|:---| | `msg` | Human-readable alert message | `msg:"ET POLICY HTTP traffic";` | | `content` | Payload pattern to match | `content:"GET /admin";` | | `nocase` | Case-insensitive match | `nocase;` | | `offset` | Skip first N bytes | `offset:4;` | | `depth` | Limit search to first N bytes | `depth:20;` | | `distance` | Bytes after previous content match | `distance:1;` | | `within` | Maximum bytes for next match | `within:10;` | | `flow` | Match only in given TCP stream state | `flow:to_server,established;` | | `classtype` | Attack category (for prioritization) | `classtype:web-application-attack;` | | `sid` | Unique Snort Rule ID | `sid:1000001;` | | `rev` | Rule revision number | `rev:1;` | | `reference` | External reference (CVE, URL) | `reference:cve,2021-44228;` | | `priority` | Severity 1 (high) → 4 (very low) | `priority:1;` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The `sid` for *local* custom rules **must** be **≥ 1,000,000**. Rules with `sid` < 1,000,000 belong to the official Talos distribution and will be overwritten on every rule update. Examiners check this! --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Configuration Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Although Snort is configuration-driven (not formula-driven), the following **decision rules and threshold formulas** form the core of the experiment's evaluative questions. | **Concept** | **Rule / Configuration / Threshold** | **Default Value** | **Unit** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Snort config file path | `/etc/snort/snort.conf` | — | — | | Local rules file | `/etc/snort/rules/local.rules` | — | — | | Custom rule SID minimum | `sid >= 1,000,000` | — | — | | Alert verbosity | `config alert_mode: full` / `fast` / `console` | `full` | — | | Packet snap length | `snaplen` in `snort.conf` | `1514` (Ethernet MTU) | bytes | | Rule application order | **First match wins** (top-to-bottom in rules file) | — | — | | PCAP block size (binary log) | `config pcap_memcap: 256` | `256` | MB | | Stream5 timeout (TCP idle) | `config policy_mode: inline` | `30` | seconds | | HTTP request body depth | `http_inspect_server.request_body_depth` | `-1` (all) | bytes | | Hyperscan engine | `config detection: search-method ac-split` | `ac-split` | — | > [!NOTE] > The classic **rule evaluation predicate** is a boolean AND across all rule options: > > $$\text{Match} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{option}_i(\text{packet})$$ > > A packet is flagged only if **every** specified option in a rule header is satisfied. If even one option fails, the rule is *not* triggered for that packet. --- ## 2.4 The `snort.conf` Configuration File — Key Variables The `snort.conf` file defines the operational parameters. The two most important variables for any lab experiment are: | Variable | Definition | Example | |:---|:---|:---| | `$HOME_NET` | The protected network (your LAN) | `var HOME_NET 192.168.1.0/24` | | `$EXTERNAL_NET` | The untrusted outside | `var EXTERNAL_NET any` | | `$HTTP_PORTS` | Web server ports | `var HTTP_PORTS 80` | | `$SQL_PORTS` | Database ports | `var SQL_PORTS 3306` | | `$RULE_PATH` | Directory of `*.rules` files | `var RULE_PATH /etc/snort/rules` | > [!TIP] > **Rule of Thumb:** When a rule fires, Snort substitutes `$HOME_NET` with your defined subnet. Always set `$HOME_NET` to *your own* network — never leave it as the default `any`, or you'll generate massive false-positive floods. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Installation, Configuration & Deployment ## 3.1 Prerequisites & Lab Environment Setup > [!NOTE] > The following procedure is the **canonical KTU lab workflow**. Perform steps sequentially — most student errors occur from skipping the dependency check. ### Step 1: System Update & Build Dependencies ```bash sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y sudo apt-get install -y build-essential libpcap-dev libpcre3-dev \ libnet1-dev zlib1g-dev luajit lua5.2-dev libdnet-dev \ libnghttp2-dev openssl libssl-dev ``` **Explanation:** Snort's core engine requires `libpcap` (packet capture), `pcre` (regex matching), `zlib` (decompression), `dnet` (network primitives), and `luajit` (Lua scripting for detection). Without these, `./configure` will fail. ### Step 2: Download & Extract Snort Source ```bash cd /tmp wget https://www.snort.org/downloads/snort/daq-2.0.7.tar.gz wget https://www.snort.org/downloads/snort/snort-2.9.20.tar.gz tar -xzf daq-2.0.7.tar.gz tar -xzf snort-2.9.20.tar.gz ``` **Explanation:** The `DAQ` (Data Acquisition) library is the abstraction layer that allows Snort to read packets from different capture backends (`pcap`, `afpacket`, `nfq`). It **must** be compiled *before* Snort itself. ### Step 3: Compile & Install DAQ ```bash cd daq-2.0.7 ./configure && make && sudo make install cd .. ``` ### Step 4: Compile & Install Snort ```bash cd snort-2.9.20 ./configure --enable-sourcefire --enable-flexible-response \ --enable-luajit --enable-perfprofiling make sudo make install sudo ldconfig ``` ### Step 5: Verify Installation ```bash snort -V ``` **Expected Output:** ``` ,,_ -*> Snort! <*- o" )~ Version 2.9.20 GRE (Build 121) '''' By Martin Roesch & The Snort Team ``` --- ## 3.2 Directory Tree Creation (The Standard FHS Layout) Snort expects a specific directory structure. Create it with: ```bash sudo mkdir -p /etc/snort/rules sudo mkdir -p /etc/snort/preproc_rules sudo mkdir -p /var/log/snort sudo mkdir -p /usr/local/lib/snort_dynamicrules sudo touch /etc/snort/rules/local.rules sudo chmod -R 5775 /etc/snort ``` | Directory | Purpose | |:---|:---| | `/etc/snort/rules` | Holds all `*.rules` files | | `/etc/snort/preproc_rules` | Preprocessor-generated dynamic rules | | `/var/log/snort` | Alert logs and PCAPs | | `/usr/local/lib/snort_dynamicrules` | Shared-object (.so) rule plugins | --- ## 3.3 Writing the `snort.conf` — The Critical Configuration File Open the config: ```bash sudo nano /etc/snort/snort.conf ``` **Key configuration directives (the ones KTU evaluates):** ```bash # === 1. Network Variables === var HOME_NET 192.168.1.0/24 var EXTERNAL_NET !$HOME_NET var HTTP_PORTS 80 var RULE_PATH /etc/snort/rules var SO_RULE_PATH /etc/snort/so_rules var PREPROC_RULE_PATH /etc/snort/preproc_rules var WHITE_LIST_PATH /etc/snort/rules var BLACK_LIST_PATH /etc/snort/rules # === 2. Decoder & Preprocessor Includes === include classification.config include reference.config # === 3. Dynamic Engine & Rules === dynamicengine /usr/local/lib/snort_dynamicengine/libsf_engine.so dynamicpreprocessor directory /usr/local/lib/snort_dynamicpreprocessor/ # === 4. Custom Local Rule (the file we created) === include $RULE_PATH/local.rules # === 5. Step 6: Test Configuration === ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Best Practice:** Always comment out the massive default `*.rules` includes in a lab session and rely *only* on `local.rules` to avoid 50,000 alerts on unrelated traffic. --- ## 3.4 Writing Custom Detection Rules — The Heart of the Lab Append custom rules to `local.rules`: ```bash sudo nano /etc/snort/rules/local.rules ``` ### Example Rule 1: ICMP Ping Detection ```snort alert icmp any any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"ICMP Ping Detected"; \ itype:8; sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:icmp-event; \ reference:arachnids,246;) ``` ### Example Rule 2: HTTP Web Shell Access ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"Possible Webshell Access"; \ flow:to_server,established; content:"/cmd.php"; nocase; \ http_uri; classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000002; rev:1; \ priority:1; reference:url,owasp.org;) ``` ### Example Rule 3: SSH Brute-Force Attempt Threshold ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"SSH Brute-Force Attempt"; \ flow:to_server,established; content:"SSH-"; offset:0; depth:4; \ detection_filter:track by_src, count 5, seconds 60; \ classtype:attempted-user; sid:1000003; rev:1;) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **`detection_filter` explained:** This is a *threshold-based* option. It suppresses the rule from firing until **5 matches from the same source IP** occur **within 60 seconds** — a classic rate-limiting anti-flood defense. --- ## 3.5 Testing Configuration Validity Before running, always validate: ```bash sudo snort -T -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 ``` The `-T` flag means **test mode**: Snort parses the config, loads the rules, and exits with a summary. **Expected Output (truncated):** ``` Snort successfully validated the configuration! Snort exiting ``` > [!WARNING] > **Failure Signatures KTU Students Encounter:** > - ❌ `ERROR: .../local.rules(0) Unable to open rules file` → **Fix:** Check `$RULE_PATH` and file permissions. > - ❌ `ERROR: Rule has no action` → **Fix:** Every rule *must* start with `alert`, `log`, or `drop`. > - ❌ `ERROR: Unknown rule option` → **Fix:** Typo in a keyword (e.g., `classytype` instead of `classtype`). --- ## 3.6 Running Snort in Live IDS Mode ```bash sudo snort -A full -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 ``` | Flag | Meaning | |:---|:---| | `-A full` | Verbose alert format (header + payload) | | `-c` | Configuration file | | `-i eth0` | Listen interface (use `ip a` to confirm) | **Alternative — logging to PCAP for later analysis in Wireshark:** ```bash sudo snort -b -l /var/log/snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 ``` --- ## 3.7 Verifying Alerts — The Proof of Work When an attack is triggered, Snort writes alerts to `/var/log/snort/alert`: ```bash sudo cat /var/log/snort/alert ``` **Sample Alert Output:** ``` [**] [1:1000001:1] ICMP Ping Detected [**] [Priority: 3] [Classification: Misc Activity] 04/15-14:32:11.234567 192.168.1.50 -> 192.168.1.10 ICMP TTL:64 TOS:0x0 ID:0 IpLen:20 DgmLen:84 DF Type:8 Code:0 ID:1024 Seq:1 ECHO ``` > [!NOTE] > **Alert Format Decoding:** > - `1:1000001:1` → **GeneratorID:RuleSID:Revision** > - `04/15-14:32:11` → Timestamp (Month/Day-Hr:Min:Sec) > - `ICMP Type:8` → Echo Request (the "ping" code) --- ## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Snort Action | Attack Scenario | Recommended Action | Why | |:---|:---|:---| | Reconnaissance (port scan) | `alert` | Inform SOC; no traffic to drop | | Malicious payload delivery | `log` | Capture evidence for forensics | | Confirmed malware C2 channel | `drop` (IPS inline) | Prevent further beaconing | | SSH brute-force (rate-limited) | `alert` + `detection_filter` | Avoid alert flood | | Webshell RCE | `drop` + `sdrop` (silent drop) | Block without notifying attacker | | Policy violation (cleartext FTP) | `alert` | Awareness, not blocking | > [!TIP] > The choice between `reject` (sends TCP RST) and `sdrop` (silent drop) is a strategic one: `reject` *alerts the attacker* that the IDS/IPS is present; `sdrop` gives them no feedback, which is better for stealth in a honeypot or deception environment. --- # 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Snort's Internal Architecture — End-to-End Packet Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Network Packets
via libpcap or afpacket] --> B[Packet Decoder
Layer 2-4 Parse] B --> C[Preprocessors
frag3, http_inspect, smtp, sfportscan] C --> D{Detection Engine
Rule Evaluation} D --> E[Match Found] D --> F[No Match] E --> G[Output Modules
alert_fast, log_pcap, syslog, unified2] F --> H[Packet Passed Silently] G --> I[Analyst Console
Wireshark, BASE, Sguil] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style D fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style H fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f style I fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40 ``` ## 4.2 Snort Rule Processing Flow — How a Single Packet Is Evaluated ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Packet Arrives] --> Q[Parse Header
IP, TCP, Ports] Q --> R{Header Matches
Rule Header?} R -- No --> X1[Skip Rule] R -- Yes --> S[Evaluate Options
content, flow, flags] S --> T{All Options Match?} T -- No --> X2[Move to Next Rule] T -- Yes --> U[Trigger Rule Action] U --> V{Action Type} V -- alert --> W1[Generate Alert Log] V -- log --> W2[Log Packet to PCAP] V -- drop --> W3[Inline Drop IPS Mode] V -- reject --> W4[Send TCP RST or ICMP Unreach] style P fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style Q fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style S fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style T fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style U fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style V fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c style X1 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f style X2 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f ``` ## 4.3 Lab Network Topology — Where to Place Snort ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INET[External Internet] ATTACKER[Attacker Host
Kali Linux] end subgraph LAN[Internal Lab Network 192.168.1.0/24] SNORT[Snort IDS
192.168.1.100
eth0 in promiscuous mode] SERVER[Target Server
192.168.1.50] CLIENT[Legitimate Client
192.168.1.10] end ATTACKER -.Nmap scan.-> SERVER ATTACKER -.SQL injection.-> SERVER CLIENT -->|Normal traffic| SERVER SNORT -.Mirrors all traffic.-> INET SNORT -.Mirrors all traffic.-> LAN style ATTACKER fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style SNORT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style SERVER fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style CLIENT fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Tap vs SPAN Port:** In a production deployment, the Snort sensor should sit on a **network TAP** (passive fiber/copper splitter) or a switch's **SPAN/mirror port**. This guarantees it sees *every* frame, including malformed ones that a switch would normally drop — essential for catching evasion attacks. ## 4.4 Rule Action Decision Tree (What Happens After a Match) ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Rule Matched] --> N{Mode of Operation} N -- IDS Passive --> O1[alert: log + notify] N -- IPS Inline --> P1{Drop or Reject} P1 -- drop --> Q1[Silently drop packet] P1 -- reject --> Q2[Send TCP RST
or ICMP unreach] P1 -- sdrop --> Q3[Drop silently
no log entry] P1 -- replace --> Q4[Replace payload content] O1 --> R1[Alert visible in
/var/log/snort/alert] Q1 --> R2[Packet discarded
attacker sees timeout] Q2 --> R3[Attacker immediately
sees connection reset] Q3 --> R4[No external signal
stealth mode] style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style N fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style P1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c style R1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style R2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style R3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style R4 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 ``` --- # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Question]** ### **Q1. Differentiate between a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) and a Network Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS).** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | NIDS (e.g., Snort in passive mode) | NIPS (e.g., Snort in inline mode) | |:---|:---|:---| | **Position** | Off-path, connected via TAP/SPAN | Inline (in the data path) | | **Action on detection** | Generates an *alert* | *Drops or rejects* the malicious packet | | **Traffic impact** | None — purely passive | Active — actively blocks flow | | **Failure mode** | Fails open (traffic continues) | Can fail closed (deny all) | | **Latency** | Near-zero | Adds a few microseconds | | **Use case** | Monitoring, SOC visibility | Perimeter enforcement | A Snort deployment in **IDS mode** operates as a NIDS; the same engine switched to **inline mode** (using `afpacket` DAQ) becomes a NIPS. *(1 mark per key distinction, 3 marks total)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Question]** ### **Q2. List and briefly explain the four major components of Snort's architecture.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** 1. **Packet Decoder (1 mark):** Receives raw bytes from the DAQ library, decodes Ethernet, IP, and TCP/UDP headers, and validates basic protocol integrity. Packets failing the decoder are silently discarded. 2. **Preprocessors (1 mark):** Plugins that normalize and reassemble traffic (e.g., `frag3` for IP fragmentation, `http_inspect` for HTTP normalization, `sfportscan` for port-scan detection). They prepare the packet for accurate rule matching. 3. **Detection Engine (1/2 mark):** The core logic unit. Compares each packet against the loaded rules using optimized multi-pattern matching (Aho-Corasick / Hyperscan). Triggers actions on matches. 4. **Output / Alerting Modules (1/2 mark):** Format and deliver alerts to external sinks: console (`alert_fast`), PCAP files (`log_tcpdump`), syslog, databases (MySQL via `Barnyard2`), or unified2 binary. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, subdivided as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer either OR choice.* --- ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Question]** #### **(a) Explain the structure of a Snort rule with a suitable example. Clearly identify each component — action, protocol, source/destination, and at least four rule options.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A Snort rule consists of two logical parts: the **rule header** and the **rule options**, both on a single line. **1. Rule Header (4 marks)** The header defines the **"who, where, and what protocol"** of the traffic to inspect. It contains six fields in order: `action protocol src_ip src_port direction dst_ip dst_port` **Reference Example:** ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 ( ... options ... ) ``` | Field | Value in Example | Meaning | |:---|:---|:---| | **Action** | `alert` | Generate an alert when matched | | **Protocol** | `tcp` | Apply to TCP packets only | | **Source IP** | `$EXTERNAL_NET` | Any IP outside the protected LAN | | **Source Port** | `any` | Any source port | | **Direction** | `->` | One-way (towards the destination) | | **Destination IP** | `$HOME_NET` | The protected LAN | | **Destination Port** | `80` | The web server port | **2. Rule Options (3 marks)** The options are enclosed in parentheses and contain the actual detection logic as `key:value` pairs separated by `;`. **Complete Example Rule:** ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"HTTP SQL Injection Attempt"; \ flow:to_server,established; content:"UNION SELECT"; nocase; \ http_uri; classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000010; rev:1; \ reference:cve,2021-12345;) ``` **Explanation of the four key options:** - **`msg:`** — Human-readable alert text shown in logs: `"HTTP SQL Injection Attempt"`. - **`flow:to_server,established`** — Match only on established TCP sessions going *to* the server (defeats blind SYN scans). - **`content:"UNION SELECT"; nocase; http_uri`** — Look for the string `UNION SELECT` in the HTTP URI, case-insensitive. This is a classic SQLi signature. - **`classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000010; rev:1`** — Categorizes the attack for prioritization; `sid` ≥ 1,000,000 confirms this is a custom user rule; `rev` tracks the version. - **`reference:cve,2021-12345`** — Links the rule to an external CVE database entry for context. > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** A rule's *header* filters the traffic stream; its *options* perform deep-packet inspection. Both must succeed for an alert to fire. --- #### **(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure to install, configure, and run Snort in IDS mode on a Linux system. Include the validation command.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Install Build Dependencies (1 mark)** ```bash sudo apt-get install -y build-essential libpcap-dev libpcre3-dev \ libnet1-dev zlib1g-dev luajit libdnet-dev ``` **Step 2 — Download & Compile DAQ then Snort (1 mark)** ```bash tar -xzf daq-2.0.7.tar.gz && cd daq-2.0.7 && \ ./configure && make && sudo make install cd ../snort-2.9.20 && ./configure && make && sudo make install ``` **Step 3 — Create Directory Structure (1 mark)** ```bash sudo mkdir -p /etc/snort/rules /var/log/snort sudo touch /etc/snort/rules/local.rules ``` **Step 4 — Configure `snort.conf` (1 mark)** Set the key variables: ```bash var HOME_NET 192.168.1.0/24 var EXTERNAL_NET !$HOME_NET var RULE_PATH /etc/snort/rules include $RULE_PATH/local.rules ``` **Step 5 — Write a Custom Detection Rule (1 mark)** ```bash echo 'alert icmp any any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"Ping Detected"; \ sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:icmp-event;)' \ | sudo tee /etc/snort/rules/local.rules ``` **Step 6 — Validate the Configuration (1 mark)** ```bash sudo snort -T -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 ``` The `-T` flag runs Snort in **test mode** — it parses the config, loads all rules, and prints `"Snort successfully validated the configuration!"` on success. **Step 7 — Run Snort in Live IDS Mode (1 mark)** ```bash sudo snort -A full -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 ``` To verify alerts, in another terminal trigger traffic (e.g., `ping` from another host) and check: ```bash sudo cat /var/log/snort/alert ``` --- ### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative to Question A) (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Question]** #### **(a) What is the role of preprocessors in Snort? Explain any three preprocessors with their configuration syntax.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Role of Preprocessors (2 marks):** Preprocessors sit *between* the packet decoder and the detection engine. They perform **normalization, reassembly, and protocol decoding** to defeat evasion techniques and prepare traffic for accurate signature matching. Without preprocessors, an attacker could obfuscate a payload (URL-encode, fragment, double-encode) and bypass the detection engine entirely. **Three Critical Preprocessors (5 marks):** **1. `frag3` — IP Defragmentation (1.5 marks)** Detects and reassembles fragmented IP packets, defeating fragmentation-overlap attacks. ```bash preprocessor frag3_global: max_frags 65536 preprocessor frag3_engine: policy bsd-right, detect_anomalies ``` **2. `http_inspect` — HTTP Normalization (1.5 marks)** Decodes HTTP requests, normalizes encoding (UTF-8, double-encoding, Unicode), and extracts URIs/headers for rules with `http_uri`, `http_header`, `http_client_body`. ```bash preprocessor http_inspect_server: server default \ profile all ports { 80 8080 8180 } \ request_body_depth 65535 \ normalize_headers \ normalize_cookies ``` **3. `sfportscan` — Port-Scan Detection (1.5 marks)** Detects reconnaissance activity (SYN scans, NULL scans, FIN scans) by tracking connection patterns from a source. ```bash preprocessor sfportscan: proto { all } \ scan_type { all } \ sense_level { medium } \ logfile { portscan.log } \ watch_ip $HOME_NET ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often forget the `_global` vs engine-specific split for `frag3`. The `_global` directive sets limits; the `_engine` directive instantiates the policy. Writing them incorrectly causes Snort to refuse to start. --- #### **(b) Demonstrate how to write a Snort rule to detect (i) an Nmap SYN scan and (ii) a Telnet login attempt. For each, explain the detection logic.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **(i) Nmap SYN Scan Detection (3.5 marks)** Nmap's classic `SYN scan` (also called *half-open scan*) sends a TCP packet with only the `SYN` flag set, *without* completing the three-way handshake. Snort detects this via the `flags` keyword. ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"Nmap SYN Scan Detected"; \ flags:S; flow:stateless; \ classtype:attempted-recon; sid:1000020; rev:1; \ reference:url,nmap.org/book/synscan.html;) ``` **Detection Logic (1 mark):** - `flags:S` → Match only packets where **only the SYN flag** is set (no ACK, no RST). - `flow:stateless` → Do not require an established session — *this is exactly what makes a half-open scan unique.* - A legitimate connection must have `flags:S` *and* expect a `flags:SA` reply; a stateless isolated `SYN` to many ports is a scan signature. **(ii) Telnet Login Attempt Detection (3.5 marks)** A Telnet login to port `23` followed by a `USER` or `login:` prompt can be detected by inspecting the payload. ```snort alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 23 (msg:"Telnet Login Attempt"; \ flow:to_server,established; content:"login|3A|"; nocase; \ classtype:attempted-user; sid:1000021; rev:1; \ priority:2;) ``` **Detection Logic (1 mark):** - Port `23` is the standard Telnet port. - `content:"login|3A|"` → The string `login:` (hex `3A` is the colon `:`), matched as raw bytes. - `nocase` → Case-insensitive match. - `flow:to_server,established` → Only fires on packets going *to* the Telnet server on an *existing* TCP connection (rules out SYN floods). - `classtype:attempted-user` → Categorized as a user-discovery attempt in the priority classification system. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `flow:stateless`** on the SYN scan rule — examiners deduct 2 marks because it becomes a duplicate of *any* SYN packet, not specifically a scan. > 2. **Using `alert` on a legitimate `flow:established` rule without `flags`** — students confuse the ACK flag, flooding logs with normal data traffic. > 3. **Picking SID < 1,000,000** for custom rules — this is a direct rule-violation per Snort's reserved-range policy; examiners deduct up to 1 mark. > 4. **Writing `flags:SA` instead of `flags:S`** for SYN scan — `SA` is a SYN+ACK reply, not a scan; this is a conceptual mistake worth losing 1.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Quick-Recall Revision Checklist ✅ > **Print this. Tape it to your lab notebook. Read it before every viva.** - **Snort is signature-based, not anomaly-based** (in its default open-source form). - **Three operating modes:** Sniffer (`-v`), Logger (`-l -b`), **Network IDS** (`-c`). - **Rule grammar:** `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (options)`. - **Five rule actions** in the header: `alert`, `log`, `pass`, `activate`, `dynamic`. With inline mode: add `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop`. - **Reserved SID range:** SIDs < 1,000,000 belong to Talos — never assign these to your own rules. - **Default config file:** `/etc/snort/snort.conf`. - **Default log directory:** `/var/log/snort/`. - **Validation flag:** `snort -T -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0`. - **Live IDS command:** `snort -A full -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0`. - **Architecture stages:** **Decoder → Preprocessors → Detection Engine → Output Modules** (memorize the order!). - **Detection engine algorithms:** Aho-Corasick (default) and Hyperscan (fast). - **`$HOME_NET` & `$EXTERNAL_NET`:** Always define them correctly — the *single most common config mistake*. - **`detection_filter`:** Used for *threshold-based* rules (e.g., 5 attempts per 60 seconds). - **`flow:to_server,established` vs `flow:stateless`:** First needs a handshake; second matches blind/half-open scans. - **PCAP output flag:** `-b` + `-l` for binary packet capture. - **DAQ library:** Must be installed *before* Snort compilation — it provides the packet capture abstraction. - **Snort ≠ Suricata:** Both are open-source NIDS, but Suricata is multi-threaded and supports more advanced scripting. Examiners sometimes ask this comparison. - **Snort was created by Martin Roesch (1998)**, now maintained by **Cisco**, licensed under **GPLv2**. - **OSI layers examined by Snort:** Primarily Layers 2 (Ethernet), 3 (IP), 4 (TCP/UDP), and 7 (HTTP/DNS/SMTP via preprocessors). - **False positive tuning:** Use `suppression` and `threshold` keywords — never just delete a rule. > 🎓 **Final KTU Pearl of Wisdom:** A well-tuned Snort deployment is one that catches the actual attack *and* stays silent during normal traffic. The art of Snort is not in writing rules — it is in **tuning thresholds, suppressing noise, and interpreting alerts with context**. ---

topics.title: Implementation of an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) using open-source tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) using open-source tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS):** A network security appliance or software that monitors network and/or system activities for malicious or policy-violating behavior, and **proactively** executes real-time prevention actions (block, drop, reset, alert) directly on the data path — unlike an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) which only *passively* raises alerts.

An IPS is the **active, in-line evolution** of an IDS. While an IDS sits off a SPAN/mirror port and merely *observes*, an IPS is placed directly in the live traffic path and can terminate offending sessions *before* the malicious payload reaches the victim host. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, IPS is positioned as a hands-on, open-source implementation topic — typically built using **Suricata** or **Snort** running in **NFQ (NetFilter Queue)** or **AF_PACKET** IPS mode on a Linux gateway.

### Formal Properties (What makes a system an "IPS"):

- **In-line operation:** Traffic physically traverses the IPS engine (often using Linux iptables + NFQUEUE).
- **Real-time blocking:** Decisions are made at wire-speed (sub-millisecond latency for typical home/lab links).
- **Stateful inspection:** Tracks TCP session state to defeat evasion via fragmentation or session splicing.
- **Protocol-aware decoding:** Parses application-layer protocols (HTTP, DNS, TLS, SMB) before rule matching.
- **Action set:** `pass`, `drop`, `reject`, `alert`, `log` (where `drop` is the defining IPS action).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Airport Security Analogy:**
> Think of a network like an **airport terminal**. A **firewall** is the *boarding-pass checker* at the entrance — it only checks your ticket (source/destination IP/port) and lets you through if it matches. An **IDS** is a *CCTV camera operator* sitting in a back room watching monitors — if he sees something suspicious, he radios security, but by then you have already boarded the plane. An **IPS** is a *uniformed security officer standing right at the metal detector* — the moment you trigger the alarm, he physically stops you from walking further. The IPS doesn't just *see* the threat; it *prevents* it in real time.

**Another simple analogy:** An IDS is a *smoke detector* that beeps when there's fire. An IPS is the *automatic sprinkler system* that detects the smoke **and** sprays water to stop the fire.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bolded for Exam Recall)

| Term | Definition | Exam Significance |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **True Positive (TP)** | Malicious traffic correctly blocked | Indicates IPS effectiveness |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Legitimate traffic wrongly dropped | Critical — causes downtime |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Malicious traffic missed | Most dangerous failure mode |
| **IPS Mode** | Inline blocking engine | Defines the system as IPS, not IDS |
| **IDS Mode** | Passive alert-only mode | Suricata/Snort default |
| **Signature (Rule)** | Pattern describing an attack | Core detection unit |
| **NFQUEUE** | Linux iptables target for in-line queueing | Enables user-space IPS |
| **ET Open Ruleset** | Emerging Threats open signature feed | Free, frequently updated |
| **Suricata** | Open-source multi-threaded IPS/IDS/NSM | Modern, default choice |
| **Snort** | Open-source IPS/IDS by Cisco (formerly Sourcefire) | Classic, rule-compatible |

## 1.4 Where IPS Fits in the **Defense-in-Depth** Model

```
Layer 7: Application Firewall / WAF
Layer 6: IPS / IDS                    ← THIS EXPERIMENT
Layer 5: NGFW (Stateful Firewall)
Layer 4: ACLs (L3/L4 Filtering)
Layer 3: Router ACL
Layer 2: Port Security / 802.1X
```

> [!NOTE]
> The IPS operates at **OSI Layers 2–7** and complements — never replaces — the firewall. A firewall decides *who* may talk; an IPS decides *what* they are saying is acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 IPS vs IDS vs Firewall — The Definitive Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **guaranteed KTU question** (either as a 3-mark direct question or as a sub-part of a 14-marker). Memorize the table below verbatim.

| Feature | Firewall | IDS (Intrusion Detection) | IPS (Intrusion Prevention) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deployment** | Inline (bridge/router) | Passive (TAP/mirror port) | **Inline (in data path)** |
| **Action on threat** | Block by IP/port rule | Log & alert only | **Block + Drop + Reset in real-time** |
| **Layer of operation** | L3–L4 mainly | L2–L7 (deep inspection) | L2–L7 (deep inspection) |
| **Latency added** | Very low (hardware) | None (passive) | Low–Medium (software) |
| **Failure mode** | Fail-open or fail-closed | Alerts lost (low risk) | **Fail-open** (recommended) |
| **Detection method** | Policy/ACL | Signature + Anomaly | Signature + Anomaly + Protocol |
| **Example tool** | iptables, pfSense | Snort (IDS mode), Zeek | **Suricata, Snort (IPS mode)** |
| **Single point of failure?** | Yes (if inline) | No | **Yes** (must be HA) |

## 2.2 The Three Detection Methodologies (Core Theory)

An open-source IPS engine (Suricata/Snort) supports three detection techniques. **KTU expects you to know all three.**

### A. Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)
- Matches packet payload/header against a database of known attack patterns.
- **Analogy:** Like matching a fingerprint in a criminal database.
- **Pros:** Very low false positives; easy to write.
- **Cons:** Cannot detect **zero-day** attacks; signatures must be updated.
- **Example rule:** `alert http any any -> any any (msg:"SQL Injection attempt"; content:"' OR 1=1--"; sid:1000001;)`

### B. Anomaly-Based Detection (Statistical / Behavioral)
- Builds a baseline of "normal" traffic; flags deviations.
- **Analogy:** A teacher who knows a student's handwriting and spots a forged signature.
- **Pros:** Can catch zero-days.
- **Cons:** Higher false positives; requires training period.
- **Example:** Unusual DNS query volume, sudden port-scan patterns.

### C. Stateful Protocol Analysis
- Understands the protocol's state machine (e.g., correct HTTP request format, valid TCP handshake).
- **Analogy:** A customs officer who knows exactly what a valid passport looks like.
- **Pros:** Detects protocol misuse (e.g., HTTP over non-standard port, malformed DNS).
- **Cons:** Computationally expensive.

## 2.3 Open-Source IPS Tools — Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Tool | Latest Stable | Rule Language | Threading | IPS Mode | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Suricata** | 7.0.x | Same as Snort (compatible) | **Multi-threaded** | NFQUEUE, AF_PACKET, DPDK | High-speed modern networks |
| **Snort 3** | 3.x | Snort rules | Multi-threaded | DAQ (NFQ, AFPacket) | Cisco-integrated environments |
| **Snort 2.9** | 2.9.x | Snort rules | Single-threaded | DAQ NFQ | Legacy / lab setups |
| **Zeek (Bro)** | 5.x | Zeek scripts (.zeek) | Multi-threaded | No native IPS (use in-line via tap) | Network Security Monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU lab exams, **Suricata** is the most-likely expected tool because: (1) modern, (2) multi-threaded, (3) supports ET Open ruleset for free, (4) easier YAML config, (5) native JSON output for SIEM integration.

## 2.4 The IPS Action Set (Suricata/Snort)

| Action | Effect | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `pass` | Allow without further inspection | Trusted internal traffic |
| `alert` | Log only, do not block | IDS mode / initial tuning |
| `drop` | Silently block & log | **IPS mode — production** |
| `reject` | Block + send RST/ICMP-unreachable | Visible attack, source should know |
| `log` | Write to log file only | Forensic-only rules |

> [!WARNING]
> In lab exams, students often confuse `alert` with `drop`. **Alert = IDS behavior; Drop = IPS behavior.** The experiment's success criterion is that the IPS actually *blocks* the attack — so the rule MUST use `drop`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Suricata rule format** | `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (msg:"..."; content:"..."; sid:...)` | `sid` must be unique > 1,000,000 for local rules |
| **Snort rule header** | `action proto src_ip src_port direction dst_ip dst_port` | Direction is `->` or `<>` |
| **Inline IPS engine** | `nfq` (legacy) or `nfqueue` (modern) | Linux iptables target |
| **Performance metric** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Packets processed}}{\text{Time}} \times \frac{\text{Bytes}}{\text{Packet}}$ | Measured in **Gbps** |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Should be < **1%** in tuned IPS |
| **Detection Rate (Recall)** | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Target > **95%** for signature-based |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Target > **99%** for production |
| **Latency (IPS)** | $L = t_{out} - t_{in}$ | Should be < **1 ms** for wire-speed |
| **NFQ mark value** | `--nfqueue 0` (queue number 0) | First queue consumed by Suricata |
| **Rule priority** | `priority:1` (highest) → `priority:4` (lowest) | Lower number = higher precedence |
| **Default IPS port for testing** | TCP 80 (HTTP), 22 (SSH), 21 (FTP) | Lab test vectors |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU does not typically ask for math derivations in the **Network Security Lab** course, but the metric formulas above (FPR, Precision, Recall) are the most likely quantitative questions. Memorize the three relationships.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Guide & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **complete, exhaustive, copy-pasteable procedure** for implementing an IPS in a KTU lab environment. Every command, every config line, and every test step is shown explicitly. **No truncation.**

## 3.1 Lab Architecture & Prerequisites

**Topology used in KTU labs:**

```
   [ Attacker Kali ]                  [ Victim Ubuntu ]
   10.10.10.50      ── SWITCH ──      10.10.10.100
                          │
                  ┌───────┴────────┐
                  │  IPS Gateway   │
                  │  Ubuntu 22.04  │
                  │  Suricata 7.0  │
                  │  eth0: inet    │
                  │  eth1: 192.168.1.1│
                  └────────────────┘
```

**Prerequisites checklist:**

1. **Host OS:** Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (or 20.04) — both work natively with Suricata.
2. **Kernel:** Linux 5.15+ (for `nf_tables` and modern NFQUEUE).
3. **Two NICs (or VLANs):** One external (internet-facing), one internal (LAN).
4. **Root/sudo access.**
5. **Internet connectivity** (to fetch ruleset).

## 3.2 Step 1 — System Update & Dependency Installation

**Step 1.1: Update package index.**

```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
```

**Step 1.2: Install Suricata and supporting packages.**

```bash
sudo apt install -y suricata jq curl iptables-persistent netfilter-persistent
```

- `jq` — JSON parser for inspecting Suricata's `eve.json` output.
- `iptables-persistent` — Saves firewall rules across reboots (critical for IPS NFQUEUE rule).
- `suricata` — The IPS engine itself (also installs `suricata-update` rule manager).

**Step 1.3: Verify installation.**

```bash
suricata --build-info
```

Expected output includes the engine version, build date, and a list of enabled features. Check that `NFQ` (Netfilter Queue) is present in the output — this confirms in-line IPS support is compiled in.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Network Interface Configuration

**Step 2.1: Identify network interfaces.**

```bash
ip a
```

You will see `eth0`, `eth1`, or `enp0s3`, `enp0s8`. Note the names.

**Step 2.2: Configure static IPs via Netplan (Ubuntu 22.04 example).**

```yaml
# /etc/netplan/01-netcfg.yaml
network:
  version: 2
  renderer: networkd
  ethernets:
    eth0:                       # external / WAN side
      dhcp4: no
      addresses: [192.168.1.10/24]
      gateway4: 192.168.1.1
    eth1:                       # internal / LAN side
      dhcp4: no
      addresses: [10.10.10.1/24]
```

Apply:

```bash
sudo netplan apply
```

## 3.4 Step 3 — The Core IPS Configuration (`suricata.yaml`)

**Step 3.1: Back up the default config.**

```bash
sudo cp /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml.bak
```

**Step 3.2: Open the config for editing.**

```bash
sudo nano /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml
```

**Step 3.3: Set the variables block (very top of file).**

```yaml
%YAML 1.1
---
# Define the networks we consider "home" (trusted / to be protected)
vars:
  address-groups:
    HOME_NET: "[10.10.10.0/24]"          # internal LAN
    EXTERNAL_NET: "![$HOME_NET]"         # everything else
  port-groups:
    HTTP_PORTS: "80"
    SSH_PORTS: "22"
```

**Step 3.4: Configure the AF_PACKET IPS section.**

Scroll to the `af-packet` section and set:

```yaml
af-packet:
  - interface: eth0                    # the NIC to inspect
    threads: 1
    defrag: yes
    cluster-type: cluster_flow
    copy-mode: ips                     # <-- THIS is what enables IPS mode
    copy-iface: eth1                   # mirror to eth1 after processing
```

- `copy-mode: ips` — **This single directive is what makes Suricata function as an IPS** (it forwards packets to the second interface after deciding drop/accept).

**Step 3.5: Enable the rule files.**

```yaml
default-rule-path: /var/lib/suricata/rules
rule-files:
  - suricata.rules
  - local.rules                        # our custom rules
```

**Step 3.6: Enable EVE JSON output (for log inspection in lab).**

```yaml
outputs:
  - eve-log:
      enabled: yes
      filetype: regular
      filename: eve.json
      types:
        - alert
        - http
        - dns
        - flow
        - tls
```

**Step 3.7: Validate configuration syntax.**

```bash
sudo suricata -T -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml -v
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `-T` flag performs a **test run** that loads config + rules and exits. **Never skip this step** — invalid rules will prevent the engine from starting. Common error: `bad rule` warnings. Fix by checking rule syntax.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Installing the Open-Source Ruleset (ET Open)

**Step 4.1: Use the bundled `suricata-update` tool.**

```bash
sudo suricata-update
```

This downloads the **Emerging Threats Open** ruleset (free, ~30,000+ rules) into `/var/lib/suricata/rules/`.

**Step 4.2: Confirm the rules loaded.**

```bash
ls -la /var/lib/suricata/rules/
sudo wc -l /var/lib/suricata/rules/*.rules
```

You should see a file named `suricata.rules` with tens of thousands of lines.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Writing a Custom Local Rule (Lab-Required)

Create a custom rule for a specific attack. This is the **core of the lab experiment** — examiners expect a custom rule.

**Step 5.1: Create `local.rules`.**

```bash
sudo nano /var/lib/suricata/rules/local.rules
```

**Step 5.2: Add a rule to detect & block an Nmap SYN scan from Kali.**

```yaml
alert http any any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"HTTP request to lab server detected"; sid:1000001; rev:1;)
drop tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"SSH brute force suspected - high packet rate"; \
  flow:to_server; flags:S; threshold:type both, track by_src, count 5, seconds 10; \
  sid:1000002; rev:1;)
drop tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 21 (msg:"FTP login attempt blocked"; \
  content:"USER"; nocase; sid:1000003; rev:1;)
```

**Step 5.3: Reload Suricata to pick up the new rule.**

```bash
sudo kill -USR2 $(pidof suricata)
```

(or `sudo systemctl reload suricata`)

**Step 5.4: Re-validate.**

```bash
sudo suricata -T -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml -v
```

## 3.7 Step 6 — Starting Suricata in IPS Mode (AF_PACKET Path)

**Step 6.1: Start as a service.**

```bash
sudo systemctl enable suricata
sudo systemctl start suricata
sudo systemctl status suricata
```

**Step 6.2: Verify it's running in IPS mode.**

```bash
ps aux | grep suricata
```

Look for `--af-packet` in the command line. If using NFQUEUE path, look for `-q 0`.

## 3.8 Step 7 — Alternative NFQUEUE IPS Method (Equally Valid)

This is the **classic iptables-NFQUEUE IPS setup**, very commonly expected in KTU viva.

**Step 7.1: Edit `suricata.yaml` to use NFQ.**

```yaml
nfq:
  - mode: accept
    repeat-mark: 1
    mark: 1/1
```

**Step 7.2: Route traffic to NFQUEUE via iptables.**

```bash
sudo iptables -I FORWARD -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 0
sudo iptables -I INPUT -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 0
```

**Step 7.3: Save iptables persistently.**

```bash
sudo netfilter-persistent save
```

**Step 7.4: Start Suricata with NFQ mode.**

```bash
sudo systemctl stop suricata
sudo suricata -q 0 -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml
```

**Step 7.5: The decision loop.**

```
Packet arrives → iptables → NFQUEUE → Suricata user-space engine
  → Verdict: ACCEPT (re-inject) | DROP (discard)
```

## 3.9 Step 8 — Testing the IPS (The Exam Demonstration)

**Test 1: Normal traffic (should pass).**

```bash
# From victim (10.10.10.100):
ping 8.8.8.8
curl http://neverssl.com
```

**Test 2: Trigger the SSH brute-force rule.**

```bash
# From attacker Kali (10.10.10.50):
nmap -sS -p 22 10.10.10.100
hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt 10.10.10.100 ssh
```

**Expected result:** Connection times out / is reset. Logs show:

```bash
sudo tail -f /var/log/suricata/eve.json | jq 'select(.event_type=="alert")'
```

You will see JSON entries with `signature_id:1000002` and `action:"blocked"`.

**Test 3: Generate a synthetic malicious payload.**

```bash
curl -A "' OR 1=1--" http://10.10.10.100/index.php
```

This should trigger a SQLi-related ET rule.

## 3.10 Step 9 — The Analytical Decision Matrix (Why Choose What)

| Lab Scenario | Recommended Action | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Just installed Suricata, observing | `alert` | Avoid false positives during tuning |
| Production LAN with mature ruleset | `drop` | Real prevention |
| Telemetry/learning only | `alert` + `log` | Forensic preservation |
| Critical infra (hospitals, banks) | `drop` + `reject` | Aggressive, but auditable |
| Unknown traffic pattern | `pass` after manual review | Whitelist false positives |
| Untrusted WAN → DMZ | `drop` | Internet-facing risks |
| Trusted LAN → Trusted LAN | `alert` | Internal monitoring |

## 3.11 Step 10 — Generating the Lab Record (Viva & Report)

**Show in your record:**

1. **Topology diagram** (use the Mermaid block from SECTION 4).
2. **`suricata.yaml` excerpt** showing `copy-mode: ips` or NFQ block.
3. **Custom `local.rules`** with at least 3 rules.
4. **Screenshots of `eve.json` alerts** before and after attack.
5. **Comparison table:** Connection success vs failure when IPS is ON vs OFF.
6. **Performance metric:** Use `sar` or `iftop` to show CPU% and throughput.

```bash
# Live throughput
iftop -i eth0
# CPU usage
top -p $(pidof suricata)
```

## 4.1 Network Topology — IPS Inline Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker Machine\nKali Linux\nIP 10.10.10.50] -->|Malicious Traffic| B{Network Switch\nUnmanaged}
    B --> C[IPS Gateway\nUbuntu + Suricata\neth0: WAN side\neth1: LAN side]
    C -->|Filtered Traffic| D[Victim Server\nUbuntu 22.04\nIP 10.10.10.100]
    C -->|Logs eve.json| E[Log Analyzer\njq / Wazuh / Splunk]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000

```

## 4.2 IPS Packet Processing Pipeline (Suricata Internal Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Packet arrives on eth0] --> P2[AF_PACKET Capture\ncopy-mode: ips]
    P2 --> P3[Packet Decoder\nL2 / L3 / L4 / L7]
    P3 --> P4{Stream Engine\nTCP State Tracking}
    P4 --> P5[Application Layer Parser\nHTTP / DNS / TLS / SMB]
    P5 --> P6[Rule Engine\nSignature Match]
    P6 --> P7{Match found?}
    P7 -->|No Match| P8[Action: PASS\nForward via eth1]
    P7 -->|Match Drop Rule| P9[Action: DROP\nPacket discarded]
    P7 -->|Match Reject| P10[Action: REJECT\nSend RST or ICMP Unreachable]
    P8 --> P11[EVE JSON Log\nForensic Record]
    P9 --> P11
    P10 --> P11

    style P1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#95e1d3
    style P9 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style P10 fill:#ffd166
    style P11 fill:#4ecdc4
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — IDS vs IPS Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Where is Suricata\ndeployed?} -->|SPAN Port\nMirror| M1[Mode: IDS\nAction: alert only\nNo traffic impact]
    S1 -->|Inline in path\nAF-PACKET copy-mode ips\nor NFQUEUE| M2[Mode: IPS\nAction: drop / reject\nReal-time blocking]

    M1 --> R1[Use case:\nMonitoring\nForensics]
    M2 --> R2[Use case:\nActive defense\nPerimeter protection]

    M2 --> Q1{Is the rule\naction drop or\nreject?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R3[Active prevention\nengine engaged]
    Q1 -->|No, alert only| R4[Still inline but\nlog-only behavior]

    style M1 fill:#ffe66d
    style M2 fill:#06d6a0,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#ffd166
```

## 4.4 Rule Anatomy — Snort/Suricata Rule Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Rule Line] --> R1[Action\nalert / drop / pass / reject]
    R --> R2[Protocol\ntcp / udp / http / dns / icmp]
    R --> R3[Source\nIP and Port]
    R --> R4[Direction\n-> or <>]
    R --> R5[Destination\nIP and Port]
    R --> R6[Rule Options\nmsg content sid rev classtype]

    style R fill:#4ecdc4
    style R1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#ffd166
    style R3 fill:#95e1d3
    style R4 fill:#a8dadc
    style R5 fill:#95e1d3
    style R6 fill:#ffe66d
```

## 4.5 End-to-End Attack-Block Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Att as Attacker (Kali)
    participant IPS as IPS Gateway (Suricata)
    participant Vic as Victim Server
    participant Log as eve.json Log

    Att->>IPS: 1. SYN packet to port 22
    activate IPS
    IPS->>IPS: 2. Decode L2-L4
    IPS->>IPS: 3. Match rule sid:1000002
    IPS-->>Att: 4. TCP RST (reject) OR Drop
    IPS->>Log: 5. Write alert JSON
    deactivate IPS

    Note over Att,IPS: Connection terminated\nat layer 4

    Att->>IPS: 6. New SSH brute-force attempt
    activate IPS
    IPS->>IPS: 7. Threshold check: count > 5 in 10s
    IPS-->>Att: 8. DROP all further packets
    IPS->>Log: 9. Log "blocked" event
    deactivate IPS

    Note over Vic: Never sees the attack
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern. Marks are split per the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** guidelines: Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** is mandatory for Part B.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). List any three distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Feature | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deployment mode** | Passive (TAP / mirror port) | **In-line** (in live data path) |
| 2 | **Action on attack** | Generates alert only | **Actively drops / blocks** the offending traffic |
| 3 | **Failure impact** | Alerts lost (low risk) | If it fails, traffic may be lost → must be **fail-open** |
| 4 | **Latency** | None (passive) | Adds small processing latency |
| 5 | **Example mode** | Snort `-A console` | Suricata with `copy-mode: ips` or NFQUEUE |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row × 3 = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is a signature in the context of an IPS? Give one example of a Suricata/Snort rule with a brief explanation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **signature** (also called a *rule*) is a pattern that describes a known attack, written in the Suricata/Snort rule language. When incoming traffic matches this pattern, the IPS triggers the configured action (`alert`, `drop`, `reject`).

**Example rule (1.5 marks):**

```yaml
drop tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"SSH Brute Force Detected"; \
  flags:S; threshold:type both, track by_src, count 5, seconds 10; \
  sid:1000001; rev:1;)
```

**Explanation (1.5 marks):**
- `drop` — action: silently block the packet.
- `tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 22` — any TCP source to HOME_NET port 22 (SSH).
- `flags:S` — match TCP SYN packets.
- `threshold: count 5, seconds 10` — alert if more than 5 SYNs in 10 seconds from the same source.
- `sid:1000001` — unique signature ID (must be > 1,000,000 for custom local rules).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in the exam. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an in-line IPS** built using open-source tools. Describe the role of `iptables` and `NFQUEUE` in routing packets to the user-space engine.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a **complete Suricata configuration snippet** that: (i) sets `HOME_NET` to `192.168.10.0/24`, (ii) captures on `eth0` in **IPS mode**, (iii) enables `eve.json` logging, and (iv) loads two rule files: `suricata.rules` and `local.rules`. Also write **one custom `drop` rule** that blocks telnet connections to HOME_NET and explain each keyword.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Attacker] ──▶ eth0 (WAN) ──▶ [iptables NFQUEUE 0] ──▶ [Suricata Engine]
                  │                                          │
                  │            Verdict: ACCEPT/DROP          │
                  └────────── eth1 (LAN) ◀───────────────────┘
                                                          │
                                                          ▼
                                                  [/var/log/suricata/eve.json]
```

**Role of iptables + NFQUEUE (4 marks):**

1. `iptables` is the Linux kernel firewall. By adding the rule `iptables -I FORWARD -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 0`, we tell the kernel: *"Do not make a forwarding decision yourself; instead, queue this packet to user-space queue 0."*
2. The packet is paused in kernel space, copied to user-space, and handed to the Suricata process.
3. Suricata's multi-threaded engine inspects the packet (decoding, signature matching, flow tracking).
4. Suricata issues a **verdict** back to the kernel: `NF_ACCEPT` (re-inject into the flow) or `NF_DROP` (discard).
5. This entire process happens at wire speed for typical lab links (< 1 ms latency).
6. The verdict is logged to `eve.json` for forensic analysis and SIEM integration.

> *(Split: 1 mark diagram, 1 mark iptables role, 2 marks Suricata verdict flow, 3 marks clarity & depth of explanation)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]**

**Part (i)–(iii): `suricata.yaml` snippet (4 marks):**

```yaml
%YAML 1.1
---
vars:
  address-groups:
    HOME_NET: "[192.168.10.0/24]"
    EXTERNAL_NET: "![$HOME_NET]"

af-packet:
  - interface: eth0
    threads: 1
    defrag: yes
    cluster-type: cluster_flow
    copy-mode: ips                # <-- enables IPS mode
    copy-iface: eth1

default-rule-path: /var/lib/suricata/rules
rule-files:
  - suricata.rules
  - local.rules

outputs:
  - eve-log:
      enabled: yes
      filetype: regular
      filename: eve.json
      types: [alert, http, dns, flow]
```

**Part (iv): Custom drop rule (2 marks):**

```yaml
drop tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 23 (msg:"Telnet connection blocked by IPS"; \
  flow:to_server,established; sid:1000010; rev:1;)
```

**Keyword explanation (1 mark):**
- `drop` — silently discard the packet (IPS behavior).
- `tcp` — protocol.
- `any any` — any source IP, any source port.
- `->` — direction (source to destination).
- `$HOME_NET 23` — destination is HOME_NET, port 23 (telnet).
- `flow:to_server,established` — only matched on established connections to the server.
- `msg:...` — human-readable alert text.
- `sid:1000010` — unique signature ID.
- `rev:1` — rule revision number.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **three detection methodologies** used by open-source IPS engines: signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis. For each, give one real-world attack example and one limitation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A lab setup has Suricata running on an Ubuntu gateway with two NICs (`eth0` = WAN, `eth1` = LAN). Describe **step-by-step** how you would: (i) install Suricata, (ii) install the ET Open ruleset, (iii) create a custom rule to block all ICMP echo requests from EXTERNAL_NET to HOME_NET, and (iv) verify the rule is working using `eve.json`.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]**

| # | Methodology | How it works | Example Attack | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Signature-Based** | Matches packet bytes/headers against a database of known attack patterns. | Detect `EICAR` test virus string in HTTP body. | **Zero-day attacks** go undetected until a signature is written. |
| 2 | **Anomaly-Based** | Learns baseline "normal" behavior, flags statistical deviations. | Detect a sudden spike in DNS queries (DNS tunneling). | **High false positive** rate; needs training period; computationally heavier. |
| 3 | **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Understands the protocol's correct state machine; flags malformed or out-of-sequence traffic. | Detect HTTP request smuggling where `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` conflict. | **Resource-intensive**; misses attacks that follow correct protocol syntax (e.g., valid SQLi). |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Modern IPS engines (Suricata/Snort 3) **combine all three** in a layered engine for maximum coverage.

*(Award: 1.5 marks per row × 4 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]**

**Step (i) — Install Suricata (1.5 marks):**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y suricata jq
suricata --build-info | grep -i nfq     # confirm NFQ support
```
