**Step (ii) — Install ET Open Ruleset (1.5 marks):**

```bash
sudo suricata-update                     # downloads Emerging Threats Open
ls -la /var/lib/suricata/rules/          # verify suricata.rules exists
wc -l /var/lib/suricata/rules/suricata.rules
```

**Step (iii) — Custom ICMP drop rule (2 marks):**

Create `/var/lib/suricata/rules/local.rules`:

```yaml
drop icmp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"ICMP Echo Request from external blocked"; \
  itype:8; sid:1000050; rev:1;)
```

- `itype:8` matches ICMP Echo Request (ping).
- `drop` ensures the packet is silently discarded.

Reload:

```bash
sudo suricata-update reload--rules
# or
sudo kill -USR2 $(pidof suricata)
```

**Step (iv) — Verification using eve.json (2 marks):**

From the attacker machine, ping the victim:

```bash
ping 10.10.10.100
```

On the IPS gateway, monitor logs in real time:

```bash
sudo tail -f /var/log/suricata/eve.json | jq 'select(.event_type=="alert" and .alert.signature_id==1000050)'
```

Expected output:

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2024-12-15T10:30:45.123456+0000",
  "flow_id": 1234567890,
  "event_type": "alert",
  "src_ip": "10.10.10.50",
  "dest_ip": "10.10.10.100",
  "proto": "ICMP",
  "alert": {
    "action": "blocked",
    "signature_id": 1000050,
    "signature": "ICMP Echo Request from external blocked",
    "category": "Potentially Bad Traffic"
  }
}
```

> The `"action": "blocked"` field confirms the IPS dropped the packet. The victim will see **0% packet loss** but no successful replies — the connection was silently severed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use `drop` in custom rules** — students write `alert` instead of `drop`. This makes Suricata behave as IDS, not IPS. **Examiner deducts 2–3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Not validating with `suricata -T`** — invalid YAML or rule syntax prevents the engine from starting. Always show this command in your record.
> 3. **Confusing `copy-mode: ips` (AF_PACKET) with NFQUEUE** — both work, but they're different mechanisms. State **which one** you used and **why**.
> 4. **Missing `flow` keyword in rules** — a rule without `flow:to_server,established` will match every packet, not just application data. This causes massive false positives.
> 5. **No comparison table** — examiners expect a "before IPS" vs "after IPS" test result table in your record.
> 6. **Forgetting IP forwarding** — if using the gateway as a router, run `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1` and add it to `/etc/sysctl.conf`.
> 7. **Using `any` for HOME_NET** — this defeats the purpose of scope definition. Always restrict to your actual subnet.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] **IPS = IDS + Active Blocking** in the live data path.
- [ ] IPS sits **in-line** (unlike IDS which is passive on a mirror port).
- [ ] The defining IPS action is **`drop`** (not `alert`).
- [ ] A **fail-open** IPS design is mandatory for production (avoid single-point-of-failure).
- [ ] **Defense-in-depth:** IPS complements, does NOT replace, the firewall.

### ✅ Detection Methodologies
- [ ] **Signature-based** → low FP, no zero-days.
- [ ] **Anomaly-based** → catches zero-days, high FP.
- [ ] **Stateful Protocol Analysis** → catches protocol misuse.
- [ ] Modern engines use **all three in layers**.

### ✅ Open-Source Tools
- [ ] **Suricata** = modern, multi-threaded, default for new deployments.
- [ ] **Snort 3** = Cisco-owned, multi-threaded, rule-compatible with Suricata.
- [ ] **Snort 2.9** = legacy, single-threaded, still in many textbooks.
- [ ] **Zeek** = NSM, not a true IPS (no native in-line blocking).
- [ ] **ET Open** = free ruleset; **ET Pro** = paid, with 0-day coverage.

### ✅ Suricata Configuration Essentials
- [ ] `vars: address-groups: HOME_NET: "[x.x.x.0/24]"`
- [ ] `af-packet: copy-mode: ips` (enables IPS via AF_PACKET).
- [ ] Alternative: `nfq:` block + `iptables -I FORWARD -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 0`.
- [ ] `outputs: - eve-log: filename: eve.json` (always-on JSON log).
- [ ] `rule-files: - local.rules` (for custom rules).

### ✅ Rule Writing (Suricata/Snort)
- [ ] Format: `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (options)`.
- [ ] `sid` **must be > 1,000,000** for local rules.
- [ ] Always include `msg:`, `sid:`, `rev:` in custom rules.
- [ ] Use `flow:to_server,established` for application-data rules.
- [ ] Use `threshold:type both, track by_src, count N, seconds S` for brute-force rules.

### ✅ IPS Modes
- [ ] **AF_PACKET IPS** — `copy-mode: ips` + `copy-iface:` → modern, kernel-bypass-friendly.
- [ ] **NFQUEUE IPS** — iptables target → classic, well-documented, slightly slower.
- [ ] **DPDK** — kernel-bypass for 10 Gbps+ deployments (advanced).

### ✅ Testing the IPS
- [ ] `nmap -sS` → port scan trigger.
- [ ] `hydra` → brute-force trigger.
- [ ] `curl -A "SQLi-string"` → web attack trigger.
- [ ] `ping` → ICMP rule trigger.
- [ ] `tail -f eve.json | jq` → live log inspection.

### ✅ Performance Metrics (Quantitative)
- [ ] $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ — Target < **1%**.
- [ ] $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — Target > **99%**.
- [ ] $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Target > **95%**.
- [ ] Latency < **1 ms** for wire-speed IPS.

### ✅ Viva-Voce Power Lines
- *"An IPS without signatures is just a stateful firewall; an IPS without deep packet inspection is just a packet filter."*
- *"The `drop` action makes a system an IPS; the `alert` action makes it an IDS."*
- *"Suricata is multi-threaded, Snort 2.9 was single-threaded — that's the key performance difference."*
- *"Fail-open is preferred over fail-closed because a network outage is more recoverable than a compromised host."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of network traffic and identification of malicious activities using IDS/IPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of network traffic and identification of malicious activities using IDS/IPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Network Security Lab (PCITL607)**, *Module Experiment 2* deals with the deployment, configuration, and analytical use of **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** and **Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS)** for live traffic analysis and malicious-activity identification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Detection System (IDS):** A security mechanism (hardware or software) that **monitors** network traffic or system activities for malicious patterns, policy violations, or known attack signatures, and **alerts** administrators when such events are detected. It operates primarily in **passive mode** (out-of-band) and does **not** directly block traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS):** An evolution of the IDS that **monitors, detects, and actively blocks** suspected malicious traffic in **real time**. It operates **inline** (in-band), sitting directly in the data path between the source and destination so that every packet traverses it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favorite):** *IDS = Watchman with a flashlight (sees and reports).* *IPS = Watchman with authority to lock the gate (sees, reports, and stops the intruder).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Airport Security Model)

Imagine the network is an **airport terminal**:

- **Firewall** = The outer perimeter wall with locked doors. It allows or denies passage based on ticket (packet header) inspection.
- **IDS** = The **CCTV control room**. Security personnel watch every camera feed (mirrored traffic) in real time. If they see a suspicious traveler (malicious payload), they radio the ground staff. They **do not** intercept the traveler.
- **IPS** = The **armed guard standing at the boarding gate**. Every passenger must pass through the metal detector (inline traffic). If a weapon (malicious packet) is detected, the guard **physically stops** the passenger from proceeding.

This analogy clarifies the operational difference: **passive observation vs. active inline intervention**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Traffic Analysis (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum analyzed packet size | $P_{max}$ | **1518 bytes** (Ethernet MTU + header) |
| Default Snort alert log file | $L_{snort}$ | **/var/log/snort/alert** |
| TCP three-way handshake packets | $N_{syn}$ | **3 packets** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) |
| Standard promiscuous mode capture filter | $F_{bpf}$ | **Berkeley Packet Filter syntax** |
| Default port-mirror (SPAN) speed | $S_{span}$ | **Line-rate (1 Gbps / 10 Gbps)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detection Methodologies

### 2.1.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)
- Compares every observed packet or session against a **predefined database of attack signatures** (e.g., Snort rules).
- **How it works:** The engine tokenizes the payload, matches it against regex patterns, and triggers an alert on a hit.
- **Why it works:** Known attacks have known byte sequences; matching is O(n) over the signature set.

### 2.1.2 Anomaly-Based Detection
- Builds a **statistical baseline** of "normal" traffic (mean packet size, inter-arrival time, protocol distribution).
- Anything deviating beyond a threshold $k\sigma$ from the baseline is flagged.
- **Why it works:** Attacks statistically distort traffic distributions.

### 2.1.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis
- Reconstructs the protocol state machine (e.g., TCP, HTTP, DNS) and verifies that observed transitions are legal.
- A packet requesting `GET /../../etc/passwd` violates HTTP semantics → flagged.

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Detection
- Combines signature + anomaly + stateful analysis to reduce both false positives and false negatives.

## 2.2 IDS vs IPS — Architectural Comparison

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Passive (tap/SPAN) | Inline (in-band) |
| Action | Alert + Log | Alert + Drop + Reset |
| Latency | Negligible | Adds **< 1 ms** per packet |
| Failure Mode | Open (fail-open) | Fail-closed (blocks all traffic if down) |
| Placement | Off the data path | In the data path |
| Examples | **Snort** (IDS mode), Suricata | **Snort-inline**, Cisco IPS, Suricata |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and tested against the **Snort 2.9.x** reference manual and RFC 5246 (TLS 1.2) which are part of the KTU recommended reading.

| Concept | Formula | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le TPR \le 1$ |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $0 \le F1 \le 1$ |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $0 \le Acc \le 1$ |
| **Bayes' Posterior (Anomaly Score)** | $P(M \mid O) = \dfrac{P(O \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(O)}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| **Z-Score Anomaly Threshold** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Flag if $\vert Z \vert > 3$ |
| **Shannon Entropy (Payload Randomness)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \le H \le 8$ bits/byte |
| **Packet Loss on Inline IPS (drops)** | $PL = \dfrac{D_{packets}}{T_{packets}}$ | Expressed as **%** |
| **TCP Sequence Anomaly Score** | $\Delta S = \vert S_{expected} - S_{observed} \vert$ | Drop if $\Delta S > T_{window}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Shannon Entropy matters in IDS:** Encrypted or obfuscated payloads (used by malware C2 channels and covert tunnels) exhibit entropy $H \approx 7.5-8$ bits/byte, while plain HTTP/HTML payloads hover at $H \approx 3-5$. A sudden entropy spike in outgoing traffic is a **high-confidence indicator of data exfiltration or covert channels** (KTU Dec 2023 question pattern).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Enterprise SOCs (Security Operations Centers)** use IDS/IPS as the **first line of behavioral defense** behind the firewall.
2. **Critical Infrastructure (SCADA/ICS networks)** deploy IPS for **protocol-aware DPI (Deep Packet Inspection)** to prevent Stuxnet-class attacks.
3. **Cloud Workloads** use virtual IPS (vIPS) inside **hypervisors** for east-west traffic inspection.
4. **Compliance** — PCI-Dss 11.4 mandates IDS/IPS deployment for any cardholder data environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes-Based Anomaly Score

The Bayesian formulation underpins most modern anomaly-based IDS engines (e.g., Snort's `threshold` preprocessor, Suricata's `threshold` rule keyword). We derive it step by step.

Let:
- $M$ = event "Packet is malicious"
- $O$ = event "Observed feature vector matches attack profile"
- $P(M)$ = *prior* probability of attack (based on historical traffic)
- $P(O \mid M)$ = *likelihood* — probability of seeing these features given an attack
- $P(O)$ = *evidence* — total probability of seeing these features

**Step 1 — State Bayes' Theorem**

$$
P(M \mid O) = \dfrac{P(O \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(O)}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the evidence using the law of total probability**

Since $O$ can occur either because of malicious traffic or benign traffic (denoted $\neg M$):

$$
P(O) = P(O \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(O \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Bayes' rule**

$$
P(M \mid O) = \dfrac{P(O \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(O \mid M) \cdot P(M) + P(O \mid \neg M) \cdot P(\neg M)}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the likelihood ratio $\Lambda$**

$$
\Lambda = \dfrac{P(O \mid M)}{P(O \mid \neg M)}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the posterior using $\Lambda$**

$$
P(M \mid O) = \dfrac{\Lambda \cdot P(M)}{\Lambda \cdot P(M) + P(\neg M)}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example**

Suppose a packet shows payload entropy $H = 7.9$ bits/byte.

- Prior probability of attack: $P(M) = 0.01$ (1% baseline attack rate).
- Likelihood of high entropy given attack: $P(O \mid M) = 0.95$ (most attacks use encryption/obfuscation).
- Likelihood of high entropy given benign: $P(O \mid \neg M) = 0.05$ (rare in normal traffic).

Compute $P(\neg M) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$.

Compute $\Lambda$:

$$
\Lambda = \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19
$$

Compute posterior:

$$
P(M \mid O) = \dfrac{19 \times 0.01}{19 \times 0.01 + 0.99} = \dfrac{0.19}{0.19 + 0.99} = \dfrac{0.19}{1.18} \approx 0.161
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

A single feature raises posterior from **1% → 16.1%**. Adding independent features (port anomaly, timing anomaly) compounds this via naive Bayes multiplication, typically pushing the posterior past the 0.85 alert threshold.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Methods vs Attack Types

| Attack Type | Signature | Anomaly | Stateful | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Known malware C2 (e.g., Zeus beacon)** | Excellent | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Zero-day exploit** | Poor | Good | Good | **Good** |
| **SQL Injection (boolean-based)** | Excellent | Poor | Excellent | **Excellent** |
| **DDoS volumetric** | Poor | Excellent | Poor | **Good** |
| **DNS tunneling** | Poor | Excellent (entropy) | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Insider data exfiltration** | Poor | Good | Poor | **Good** |

## 3.3 Confusion Matrix Construction (for Lab Evaluation)

The KTU lab record must include a confusion matrix computed from Snort alerts versus ground truth. Derive each cell:

$$
TP = \text{attacks correctly flagged by IDS}
$$

$$
FP = \text{benign packets wrongly flagged}
$$

$$
TN = \text{benign packets correctly ignored}
$$

$$
FN = \text{attacks the IDS missed}
$$

Then evaluate the metric set from Section 2.3 directly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Architecture with IDS and IPS Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INTERNET EDGE] --> B[PERIMETER FIREWALL]
    B --> C[IPS INLINE ENGINE]
    C --> D[CORE L3 SWITCH]
    D --> E[SERVER FARM ZONE]
    D --> F[USER LAN ZONE]
    D --> G[SPAN PORT MIRROR]
    G --> H[IDS SENSOR PASSIVE]
    H --> I[SIEM LOG AGGREGATOR]
    I --> J[SOC ANALYST DASHBOARD]
    C -.DROP ALERT.-> I
    H -.DETECT ALERT.-> I
    I -.CORRELATE.-> J
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- Packets traverse **FIREWALL → IPS → SWITCH** because IPS is inline.
- The switch **mirrors** a copy of all traffic to a **SPAN port**, which the IDS reads **passively**.
- Both IPS drops and IDS alerts feed the **SIEM**, where correlation rules fire.

## 4.2 Snort Rule Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[RAW PACKET FROM NIC] --> P1[DECODE L2 L3 L4 HEADERS]
    P1 --> P2[PREPROCESSORS NORMALIZE]
    P2 --> P3[DETECTION ENGINE MATCH RULES]
    P3 --> P4{RULE MATCH}
    P4 -- YES --> P5[ACTION LOG ALERT DROP REJECT]
    P4 -- NO --> P6[FORWARD PACKET IPS ONLY]
    P5 --> P7[OUTPUT PLUGIN UNIFIED2 SYSLOG]
    P6 --> P8[END OF PIPELINE]
    P7 --> P8
```

**Explanation of Pipeline:**
1. **Decode stage** reconstructs Ethernet/IP/TCP/UDP layers.
2. **Preprocessors** reassemble fragments, detect port scans, normalize HTTP.
3. **Detection engine** runs Aho-Corasick string matching against rule patterns.
4. **Action stage** executes the rule's `alert` / `drop` / `reject` directive.

## 4.3 Snort Rule Format (KTU Lab Reference)

```
ACTION  PROTOCOL  SRC_IP  SRC_PORT  DIRECTION  DST_IP  DST_PORT  OPTIONS
alert   tcp       any     any        ->         any     80        (msg:"HTTP GET with SQLi"; content:"UNION SELECT"; nocase; sid:1000001;)
```

| Field | Purpose | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| **action** | What to do on match | `alert`, `log`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop` |
| **protocol** | L4 protocol | `tcp`, `udp`, `icmp`, `ip` |
| **src/dst** | Endpoint spec | `any`, `$HOME_NET`, `192.168.1.0/24` |
| **direction** | Flow direction | `->` (unidirectional), `<>` (bidirectional) |
| **options** | Detection payload | `msg`, `content`, `nocase`, `sid`, `classtype` |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between an IDS and an IPS with respect to placement, action taken, and failure mode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Placement** | Out-of-band (passive tap or SPAN port) | Inline (in-band, on the data path) |
| **Action** | Generates alerts and logs only | Generates alerts **and** drops/blocks packets |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-open (traffic flows even if IDS fails) | Fail-closed (traffic blocked if IPS fails) |

*(1 mark each — full marks for correct comparison across all three rows.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**What is a false positive in the context of an IDS? How does a high false-positive rate impact a Security Operations Center?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **false positive (FP)** is an alert raised by the IDS for traffic that is actually **benign** (1 mark).
- It is computed as $FP = FP / (FP + TN)$ (1 mark).
- A **high FPR** causes **alert fatigue**, where analysts start ignoring alerts, leading to **real attacks being missed** (false negatives increase). It also wastes storage and CPU on log processing (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the three primary detection methodologies used in IDS: signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis. Compare them on a 4-point matrix.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Signature-Based Detection**
- Uses a database of pre-compiled attack patterns (signatures).
- Matches packet payload bytes against rule patterns (e.g., Snort `content:"/bin/sh"`).
- *Strength:* Near-zero false positives for known attacks.
- *Weakness:* Cannot detect zero-day or polymorphic attacks.

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection**
- Learns a baseline of normal traffic (mean, variance, entropy).
- Flags statistical deviations (e.g., $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma > 3$).
- *Strength:* Detects novel attacks.
- *Weakness:* Higher false-positive rate; requires a "training period".

**3. Stateful Protocol Analysis**
- Reconstructs the protocol state machine (TCP, HTTP, DNS, SMTP).
- Verifies that observed transitions are legal per RFC.
- *Strength:* Detects semantic violations (e.g., HTTP request smuggling).
- *Weakness:* Computationally expensive; requires per-protocol decoders.

**4-Point Comparison Matrix (4 marks for the table itself):**

| Criterion | Signature | Anomaly | Stateful |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detects known attacks | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Detects zero-day | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| False Positive Rate | Low | Medium-High | Low |
| Computational Cost | Low | Medium | High |

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks per detection method description, 1 mark for table.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Consider an IDS that processes 10,000 packets. The ground truth and IDS output are:**
- Attacks correctly flagged (TP) = 80
- Benign correctly ignored (TN) = 9,100
- Benign wrongly flagged (FP) = 20
- Attacks missed (FN) = 800

**Compute: TPR, FPR, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score. Comment on the IDS performance.**

**Model Solution (step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Write given values**

$$
TP = 80, \quad TN = 9100, \quad FP = 20, \quad FN = 800
$$

$$
Total = TP + TN + FP + FN = 80 + 9100 + 20 + 800 = 10000 \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute True Positive Rate (Recall)**

$$
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 800} = \dfrac{80}{880} \approx 0.0909 \approx 9.09\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute False Positive Rate**

$$
FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{20}{20 + 9100} = \dfrac{20}{9120} \approx 0.00219 \approx 0.22\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Precision**

$$
P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Accuracy**

$$
Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{Total} = \dfrac{80 + 9100}{10000} = \dfrac{9180}{10000} = 0.918 = 91.8\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.80 \times 0.0909}{0.80 + 0.0909}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.0727}{0.8909} = 2 \cdot 0.0816 = 0.1632 \approx 16.32\%
$$

**Step 7 — Performance Commentary (1 mark)**
- Although **accuracy = 91.8%** looks high, the **Recall = 9.09%** is disastrous — the IDS is missing **90% of real attacks**.
- The **F1-Score = 16.32%** confirms weak overall performance.
- **Conclusion:** This is a classic *accuracy paradox* case; the IDS must be retrained with better signatures or anomaly thresholds.

*(1 mark per major computation step, 1 mark for commentary.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical network IDS deployment. Where should the IDS sensor be placed, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (description for sketch — 3 marks):**

```
INTERNET → FIREWALL → [SPAN/MIRROR] → CORE SWITCH → INTERNAL NETWORK
                              ↓
                         IDS SENSOR (Passive)
                              ↓
                         SIEM / LOG SERVER
                              ↓
                         SOC DASHBOARD
```

**Placement Options (2 marks each):**

1. **Behind the firewall (inside the perimeter):** Most common. The IDS monitors traffic that has already passed the firewall, so it sees only *traffic that succeeded the first line of defense* — useful for catching internal compromises and lateral movement.

2. **In front of the firewall (DMZ-edge):** Captures *all* inbound traffic including scans, probes, and attacks blocked by the firewall. Useful for threat intelligence but generates high alert volume.

3. **Inside critical subnets:** Sensors placed near servers/databases to detect east-west attacks and insider threats.

**Why passive placement (2 marks):**
- IDS must not become a single point of failure.
- A passive sensor on a SPAN port sees traffic but does not modify it.
- If the IDS crashes, the network keeps functioning.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Snort rule format with a suitable example. How does Snort perform signature matching using the Aho-Corasick algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

**Snort Rule Format (3 marks):**

```
<action> <proto> <src_ip> <src_port> <dir> <dst_ip> <dst_port> (<options>)
```

**Example Rule (2 marks):**

```
alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET 80 (msg:"Possible SQL Injection Attempt"; content:"UNION SELECT"; nocase; http_uri; sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:web-application-attack;)
```

- **action** = `alert` (log but do not drop)
- **proto** = `tcp`
- **endpoints** = internal → external on port 80
- **content** = matches the literal string `UNION SELECT` (case-insensitive) inside the HTTP URI

**Aho-Corasick Matching (2 marks):**
- Snort's detection engine compiles all loaded rule `content` patterns into a **single finite automaton** (trie + failure links) at startup.
- Each input byte triggers an O(1) state transition.
- This allows matching **thousands of patterns in a single pass** over the payload — an order of magnitude faster than naive per-pattern matching.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Points**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS placement.** Students often draw IPS off the data path. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to write units** (e.g., accuracy as a percentage vs decimal). Lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the "Total = 10000" verification step** in confusion matrix problems. Lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Writing Snort rules without the semicolon-terminated options block.** Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to comment** on the computed metrics. Lose 1 mark in 7-mark computational questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- ✅ **IDS = passive, alert-only; IPS = inline, alert + block.**
- ✅ **Signature-based** = low FP, zero-day blind. **Anomaly-based** = high FP, zero-day capable.
- ✅ **TPR** = $TP / (TP + FN)$, **FPR** = $FP / (FP + TN)$, **F1** = harmonic mean of P and R.
- ✅ **Snort rule skeleton:** `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (msg:; content:; sid:; classtype:)`
- ✅ **Shannon entropy** of payload is the **golden metric** for detecting encrypted/exfiltrated data.
- ✅ **IDS placement** = SPAN port (passive mirror). **IPS placement** = inline between firewall and core switch.
- ✅ **Aho-Corasick** = multi-pattern matcher used by Snort to match thousands of signatures in O(n).
- ✅ **Accuracy paradox:** High accuracy ≠ good IDS. Always check Recall and F1.
- ✅ **Bayes' theorem** updates posterior attack probability as new features are observed.
- ✅ **Fail-open vs Fail-closed:** IDS fails open (network safe); IPS fails closed (network safe, but blocked).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration of a network firewall using iptables/pfSense
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Configuration of a network firewall using iptables/pfSense # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: CONFIGURATION OF A NETWORK FIREWALL USING iptables / pfSense ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Firewall (KTU 2024 Definition):** A network firewall is a security device (hardware or software) that monitors, filters, and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security rules, establishing a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks. A firewall operates on the principle of **packet filtering**, **stateful inspection**, and **proxy services** to enforce an organization's security policy. In the KTU Network Security Lab (PCITL607), two industry-standard firewall implementations are prescribed: - **iptables** — the legacy Linux kernel-level packet filtering framework (netfilter hooks). - **pfSense** — a FreeBSD-based, open-source graphical firewall distribution built on **PF (Packet Filter)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Guard Imagine a high-rise office building: | Real-World Element | Firewall Equivalent | |---|---| | The main entrance gate | Network interface (NIC) | | The security guard | Firewall rule engine | | The visitor's ID card | Packet header (IP, Port, Protocol) | | The building's access policy | Rule chain (ACCEPT/DROP/REJECT) | | The visitor logbook | Connection tracking table (`conntrack`) | | The guard's notebook of "known faces" | Stateful inspection database | Just as a guard checks *who you are*, *where you came from*, and *whether you are on the approved list* before letting you in, a firewall inspects packet headers, source/destination addresses, ports, and connection states before deciding the packet's fate. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > - **Packet:** A formatted unit of data carried by a network. > - **Rule:** A conditional statement that matches packet fields and defines an action. > - **Chain:** An ordered list of rules traversed sequentially. > - **Table:** A collection of chains organized by packet-processing function. > - **Default Policy:** The action taken when no rule matches (`ACCEPT` or `DROP`). > - **Stateful Inspection:** Tracking the state of network connections across packets. > - **NAT (Network Address Translation):** Rewriting source/destination addresses of packets. --- ## 1.4 Introduction to iptables `iptables` is a userspace command-line utility that configures the **netfilter** framework inside the Linux kernel. Every packet that traverses a Linux machine passes through a series of netfilter hooks, where iptables rules are evaluated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architecture:** `iptables` → `netfilter` (kernel) → NIC driver. Rules are stored in memory and applied at the kernel level for high performance. ### 1.5 Introduction to pfSense `pfSense` is a free, open-source firewall distribution derived from **FreeBSD** and the **pf (Packet Filter)** firewall. It is administered through a **web-based GUI** and is widely deployed in small-to-medium enterprise networks. **Key features:** - Web-based configuration interface - Stateful packet filtering - NAT, VPN (IPsec, OpenVPN, WireGuard) - Traffic shaping (QoS) - Captive portal, DHCP, DNS forwarding - High Availability (CARP) --- # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 iptables Architecture — Tables, Chains, and Rules The iptables framework is organized as a **hierarchical structure of tables → chains → rules**. ### 2.1.1 The Five Built-in Tables > [!NOTE] > A **table** groups chains according to the *type* of packet processing they perform. | Table Name | Purpose | Built-in Chains | |---|---|---| | **filter** | Default table for packet filtering (allowing/blocking) | INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD | | **nat** | Network Address Translation (SNAT, DNAT, MASQUERADE) | PREROUTING, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING | | **mangle** | Specialized packet alteration (TOS, TTL, MARK) | All five chains | | **raw** | Connection-tracking exemption | PREROUTING, OUTPUT | | **security** | SELinux context marking (SMACK, AppArmor) | INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD | ### 2.1.2 The Five Built-in Chains (Netfilter Hooks) A **chain** is a sequence of rules attached to a specific netfilter hook point in the packet traversal path. | Chain | Netfilter Hook | When Triggered | |---|---|---| | **PREROUTING** | NF_INET_PRE_ROUTING | Immediately after packet enters, before routing decision | | **INPUT** | NF_INET_LOCAL_IN | After routing, destined for a local process | | **FORWARD** | NF_INET_FORWARD | After routing, destined for another host (routing) | | **OUTPUT** | NF_INET_LOCAL_OUT | Generated by local process, before routing | | **POSTROUTING** | NF_INET_POST_ROUTING | Just before packet leaves the system | ### 2.1.3 Packet Flow Diagram (Logical Order) 1. Packet arrives at NIC → **PREROUTING** (raw → mangle → nat) 2. Routing decision: *local?* or *forward?* 3. If **local** → **INPUT** (mangle → filter → security) → local socket 4. If **forward** → **FORWARD** (mangle → filter → security) → **POSTROUTING** 5. Locally generated packet → **OUTPUT** (raw → mangle → nat → filter → security) → **POSTROUTING** 6. Packet leaves via NIC ### 2.2 Rule Syntax — The Heart of iptables > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generic Rule Format:** > `iptables [-t table] COMMAND CHAIN MATCH -j TARGET` #### 2.2.1 COMMAND (Rule Management Operations) | Command | Short | Function | |---|---|---| | `--append` | `-A` | Append rule to end of chain | | `--insert` | `-I` | Insert rule at given position | | `--delete` | `-D` | Delete a rule | | `--replace` | `-R` | Replace a rule | | `--list` | `-L` | List all rules in chain | | `--flush` | `-F` | Delete all rules in chain | | `--zero` | `-Z` | Zero packet/byte counters | | `--policy` | `-P` | Set default policy for chain | | `--new-chain` | `-N` | Create user-defined chain | | `--rename-chain` | `-E` | Rename a user-defined chain | #### 2.2.2 MATCH (Packet Matching Criteria) | Match Module | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | `-p` / `--protocol` | IP protocol | `-p tcp`, `-p udp`, `-p icmp` | | `-s` / --source | Source IP/network | `-s 192.168.1.0/24` | | `-d` / --destination | Destination IP/network | `-d 10.0.0.5` | | `--sport` / `--source-port` | Source port | `--sport 1024:65535` | | `--dport` / `--destination-port` | Destination port | `--dport 22` | | `-i` / --in-interface | Input interface | `-i eth0` | | `-o` / --out-interface | Output interface | `-o eth1` | | `-m state` | Connection state | `-m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED` | | `-m multiport` | Multiple ports | `-m multiport --dports 80,443` | | `-m mac` | MAC address | `-m mac --mac-source 00:11:22:33:44:55` | | `-m limit` | Rate limiting | `-m limit --limit 5/min` | #### 2.2.3 TARGET (Action to Take on Match) | Target | Action | |---|---| | `ACCEPT` | Allow the packet to proceed | | `DROP` | Silently discard the packet (no response) | | `REJECT` | Discard and send ICMP error back to sender | | `LOG` | Log the packet and continue processing | | `SNAT` | Source NAT (rewrite source IP) | | `DNAT` | Destination NAT (rewrite destination IP) | | `MASQUERADE` | Special SNAT for dynamic IPs (e.g., PPPoE) | | `RETURN` | Return to calling chain | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — iptables > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every command a KTU lab viva may test. | # | Task | Command | |---|---|---| | 1 | List all rules with line numbers | `iptables -L -n -v --line-numbers` | | 2 | Set default policy to DROP | `iptables -P INPUT DROP` | | 3 | Allow loopback | `iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT` | | 4 | Allow established/related | `iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT` | | 5 | Allow SSH from specific subnet | `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.1.0/24 --dport 22 -j ACCEPT` | | 6 | Block ping (ICMP) | `iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j DROP` | | 7 | Enable IP forwarding | `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` | | 8 | Enable NAT (MASQUERADE) | `iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE` | | 9 | Port forwarding (DNAT) | `iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 8080 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.1.10:80` | | 10 | Save rules (Debian/Ubuntu) | `netfilter-persistent save` | | 11 | Save rules (RHEL/CentOS) | `service iptables save` | | 12 | Log dropped packets | `iptables -A INPUT -j LOG --log-prefix "DROPPED: "` | ### 2.4 pfSense Architecture pfSense is structured around several core components: | Component | Function | |---|---| | **FreeBSD Base** | Underlying OS providing PF, ALTQ, CARP | | **pf (Packet Filter)** | Stateful firewall engine | | **Web Configurator** | PHP-based GUI on port 443 (HTTPS) | | **Config.xml** | Centralized XML configuration file | | **Services** | DHCP, DNS, VPN, Captive Portal, etc. | | **Packages** | Add-on modules (Snort, Suricata, pfBlockerNG) | ### 2.5 pfSense Rule Processing Order > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** pfSense follows a **first-match-wins** model. Rules are evaluated **top-to-bottom**; the first matching rule is applied, and processing stops. **Default behavior:** pfSense blocks all traffic on WAN and allows all traffic on LAN — this is the opposite of pure iptables, where the default is permissive. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Enterprise Perimeter Defense:** Blocking malicious traffic at the network edge. - **Server Hardening:** Restricting SSH, RDP, and database ports to specific IPs. - **Multi-tenant Isolation:** Segmenting VLANs in cloud/hosted environments. - **Compliance:** Meeting PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 requirements. - **IoT Security:** Isolating untrusted IoT devices on a guest VLAN. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Packet Decision Logic Every packet entering the firewall undergoes a deterministic decision process. We derive the boolean expression that governs the **final disposition** of a packet. ### 3.1.1 Rule Evaluation Function Let there be $n$ rules in a chain: $R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n$. For a given packet $P$, define the matching function: $$ M(R_i, P) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if packet } P \text{ satisfies all match conditions of rule } R_i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The **first-match-wins** policy can be expressed as: $$ \text{Match Index} \; k = \min_{i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}} \left\{ i \mid M(R_i, P) = 1 \right\} $$ The **disposition** of packet $P$ is therefore: $$ D(P) = \begin{cases} \text{TARGET}(R_k) & \text{if such } k \text{ exists} \\ \text{DEFAULT\_POLICY}(C) & \text{if no rule matches} \end{cases} $$ where $C$ is the chain under evaluation, and $\text{TARGET}(R_k) \in \{\text{ACCEPT}, \text{DROP}, \text{REJECT}, \text{LOG}, \dots\}$. ### 3.1.2 Connection State Tracking iptables' stateful module classifies packets into four states: | State | Definition | |---|---| | `NEW` | The first packet of a connection (SYN) | | `ESTABLISHED` | Packets belonging to an existing connection | | `RELATED` | New connection related to an existing one (e.g., FTP data) | | `INVALID` | Packets that don't belong to any known connection | Mathematically, a packet is classified by the tuple: $$ S(P) = (sIP, sPort, dIP, dPort, proto) $$ The connection-tracking table $T$ stores active flows: $$ T = \{ (S_1, \text{state}_1), (S_2, \text{state}_2), \dots \} $$ A new packet's classification is: $$ S(P) = \begin{cases} \text{NEW} & \text{if } S(P) \notin T \text{ and } P \text{ is a SYN} \\ \text{ESTABLISHED} & \text{if } S(P) \in T \text{ and reverse tuple also } \in T \\ \text{RELATED} & \text{if } S(P) \notin T \text{ but } P \text{ is associated with an entry} \\ \text{INVALID} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### 3.1.3 NAT Address Transformation Equation For **Source NAT (SNAT)**, the source IP is rewritten: $$ sIP' = f_{SNAT}(sIP, \text{out-interface}) $$ For **Destination NAT (DNAT)**, the destination IP/port is rewritten before routing: $$ dIP' = f_{DNAT}(dIP, dPort) $$ For **MASQUERADE** (a dynamic SNAT used with interfaces having variable IPs): $$ sIP' = IP_{out-interface} \quad \text{(queried at packet departure time)} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example — Deriving a Complete Firewall Policy **Scenario:** A Linux server (eth0 = public, eth1 = LAN) must: 1. Block all inbound traffic by default. 2. Allow SSH (port 22) only from 192.168.1.0/24 on eth1. 3. Allow HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) from anywhere on eth0. 4. Allow established and related return traffic. 5. Allow the server to ping the outside world. 6. Enable IP forwarding and NAT for the LAN subnet. **Step-by-step derivation of rules:** **Step 1 — Flush old rules and set defaults:** ```bash iptables -F iptables -P INPUT DROP iptables -P FORWARD DROP iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT ``` **Step 2 — Allow loopback (essential for local services):** ```bash iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 3 — Allow established/related inbound (stateful return traffic):** ```bash iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 4 — Allow SSH only from the LAN subnet:** ```bash iptables -A INPUT -i eth1 -p tcp -s 192.168.1.0/24 --dport 22 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 5 — Allow web traffic from anywhere on the public interface:** ```bash iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 80 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 443 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 6 — Allow outbound ping (ICMP):** ```bash iptables -A OUTPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-reply -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 7 — Enable IP forwarding and NAT for LAN → WAN:** ```bash echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.1.0/24 -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE ``` **Step 8 — Allow LAN-to-WAN forwarding (for the LAN clients to reach the internet):** ```bash iptables -A FORWARD -i eth1 -o eth0 -s 192.168.1.0/24 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT iptables -A FORWARD -i eth0 -o eth1 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT ``` **Step 9 — Verify and save:** ```bash iptables -L -n -v --line-numbers netfilter-persistent save ``` ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — iptables vs pfSense | Feature | iptables (Linux) | pfSense (FreeBSD/PF) | |---|---|---| | **Platform** | Linux kernel | FreeBSD-based appliance/VM | | **Interface** | Command-line | Web GUI + CLI | | **Default Rule** | ACCEPT (must be hardened) | Block WAN, Allow LAN | | **Stateful Engine** | `conntrack` | `pf` state table | | **NAT Types** | SNAT, DNAT, MASQUERADE | rdr (DNAT), nat (SNAT) | | **Rule Logic** | First-match-wins | First-match-wins | | **Tables** | 5 built-in tables | 2 main: filter, nat | | **User-defined Chains** | Yes | No (uses aliases/schedules) | | **Aliases/Objects** | Limited | First-class IP/port aliases | | **Logging** | syslog/klogd | Built-in GUI log viewer | | **High Availability** | Keepalived/VRRP | CARP (native) | | **Best Use Case** | Linux servers, cloud VMs | Network perimeter, SMB gateways | | **Configuration File** | `/etc/iptables/rules.v4` | `/conf/config.xml` | ### 3.4 Rule Order Derivation — Why Order Matters Given the first-match-wins property, we derive the canonical rule ordering principle: > **Most-specific rules must precede general rules.** Formally, if rule $R_a$ has match set $\mathcal{M}_a$ and rule $R_b$ has match set $\mathcal{M}_b$, and $\mathcal{M}_a \subset \mathcal{M}_b$ (i.e., $R_a$ is more specific), then the index ordering must satisfy: $$ k_a < k_b $$ **Counter-example (incorrect ordering):** ```bash # WRONG: This general DROP rule will match SSH traffic first iptables -A INPUT -j DROP iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT ``` **Correct ordering:** ```bash # RIGHT: Specific ACCEPT rule evaluated first iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT iptables -A INPUT -j DROP ``` --- # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 iptables Packet Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Packet Arrives at NIC] --> B[PREROUTING Chain] B --> B1[raw table] B1 --> B2[mangle table] B2 --> B3[nat table - DNAT] B3 --> C{Routing Decision} C -->|Local Delivery| D[INPUT Chain] C -->|Forward to Other Host| E[FORWARD Chain] C -->|Locally Generated| F[OUTPUT Chain] D --> D1[mangle] D1 --> D2[filter] D2 --> D3[security] D3 --> G[Local Socket / Process] E --> E1[mangle] E1 --> E2[filter] E2 --> E3[security] E3 --> H[POSTROUTING Chain] F --> F1[raw] F1 --> F2[mangle] F2 --> F3[nat] F3 --> F4[filter] F4 --> F5[security] F5 --> H H --> H1[nat - SNAT/MASQUERADE] H1 --> H2[mangle] H2 --> I[Packet Leaves via NIC] G --> J[Local Process Generates Response] J --> F ``` ## 4.2 pfSense Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WAN_NET["WAN / Internet"] WAN[Internet Cloud] end subgraph PFSENSE_FW["pfSense Firewall Appliance"] WAN_IF[WAN Interface] FW_ENGINE[PF Packet Filter Engine] STATE_TBL[State Table] LAN_IF[LAN Interface] OPT_IF[OPT Interface VLAN] end subgraph LAN_NET["LAN / Internal Network"] LAN_PC[Workstation 192.168.1.10] SERVER[Server 192.168.1.20] DMZ_HOST[DMZ Web Server 192.168.2.5] end WAN --> WAN_IF WAN_IF --> FW_ENGINE FW_ENGINE <--> STATE_TBL FW_ENGINE --> LAN_IF FW_ENGINE --> OPT_IF LAN_IF --> LAN_PC LAN_IF --> SERVER OPT_IF --> DMZ_HOST style FW_ENGINE fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff style STATE_TBL fill:#ffd93d,color:#000000 style WAN_IF fill:#4ecdc4 style LAN_IF fill:#95e1d3 style OPT_IF fill:#a8e6cf ``` ## 4.3 Rule Processing Logic Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Incoming Packet] --> Q{For each Rule in Chain} Q -->|Rule Matches| R[Execute TARGET Action] Q -->|No Match| S{Next Rule?} S -->|Yes| Q S -->|No - End of Chain| T{Default Policy} R --> R1[ACCEPT] R --> R2[DROP] R --> R3[REJECT] R --> R4[LOG and Continue] T -->|ACCEPT| R1 T -->|DROP| R2 R1 --> X[Packet Proceeds] R2 --> Y[Packet Silently Discarded] R3 --> Z[ICMP Error Sent] R4 --> Q style R1 fill:#2ecc71,color:#ffffff style R2 fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff style R3 fill:#e67e22,color:#ffffff style R4 fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Lab Topology — Typical KTU Network Security Lab Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LAB["KTU Lab Topology"] ATTACKER[Attacker / Kali Linux
IP 10.0.0.50] FW[Firewall Host
iptables OR pfSense VM
eth0 - 10.0.0.1 / WAN
eth1 - 192.168.1.1 / LAN] SERVER1[Web Server
192.168.1.20] SERVER2[SSH Server
192.168.1.30] CLIENT[LAN Client
192.168.1.100] end ATTACKER <-->|Traffic to be filtered| FW FW <--> SERVER1 FW <--> SERVER2 FW <--> CLIENT style FW fill:#ff4757,color:#ffffff style ATTACKER fill:#2f3542,color:#ffffff style SERVER1 fill:#1e90ff,color:#ffffff style SERVER2 fill:#1e90ff,color:#ffffff style CLIENT fill:#7bed9f ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between `DROP` and `REJECT` targets in iptables. When would you prefer one over the other?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `DROP` | `REJECT` | |---|---|---| | Action | Silently discards the packet | Discards and sends an ICMP error (e.g., "port unreachable") back to sender | | Sender Feedback | No response — appears as a timeout | Immediate error — sender knows the port is closed | | Stealth | More secure (hides existence of service) | Reveals the host is up | | Performance | Marginally faster (no ICMP generation) | Slightly slower (ICMP generation overhead) | | Use Case | Production servers, exposed hosts | Internal networks, troubleshooting | **Preferred scenarios:** Use `DROP` for production internet-facing firewalls to avoid leaking topology information to attackers. Use `REJECT` on internal/trusted networks for faster error reporting and better user experience. --- ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **What is the difference between SNAT and MASQUERADE in iptables? Provide a practical example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **SNAT (Source NAT):** Rewrites the source IP to a **specific static IP address**. Used when the outgoing interface has a fixed IP. - **MASQUERADE:** A special, dynamic form of SNAT that automatically uses the **current IP of the outgoing interface**. Used when the IP is dynamic (e.g., DHCP, PPPoE). **Examples:** ```bash # SNAT — for a static public IP 203.0.113.5 iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j SNAT --to-source 203.0.113.5 # MASQUERADE — for a dynamically assigned IP iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE ``` MASQUERADE has slight overhead because it queries the interface IP for every packet, but it is essential when the public IP changes. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (`a` and `b`). --- ### Question B-A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the complete architecture of iptables including all built-in tables, chains, and the order in which a packet traverses them. Draw a neat labeled diagram of the packet flow. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the iptables rules to configure a Linux server acting as a gateway/firewall with the following requirements: **(7 marks)** 1. Default policies: DROP for INPUT and FORWARD; ACCEPT for OUTPUT. 2. Allow loopback traffic. 3. Allow SSH (port 22) from the LAN subnet `192.168.10.0/24` only. 4. Allow HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) from anywhere. 5. Block ping (ICMP echo-request) from external network but allow LAN to ping. 6. Enable IP forwarding and configure MASQUERADE NAT for the LAN. #### Model Solution: **(a) — Architecture (7 marks):** iptables is the userspace tool for managing the **netfilter** framework in the Linux kernel. It is organized into **5 tables**, each containing **chains** which are sequences of **rules**. **Built-in Tables:** 1. **filter** — Default table for allowing/blocking packets. 2. **nat** — Network Address Translation (DNAT, SNAT, MASQUERADE). 3. **mangle** — Specialized packet header modifications (TOS, TTL, MARK). 4. **raw** — Connection-tracking exemption (NOTRACK target). 5. **security** — SELinux/SMACK mandatory access control marking. **Built-in Chains (5 netfilter hooks):** 1. **PREROUTING** — Triggered immediately after packet ingress, before routing. 2. **INPUT** — Triggered for packets destined to local sockets. 3. **FORWARD** — Triggered for packets being routed through the host. 4. **OUTPUT** — Triggered for locally generated packets. 5. **POSTROUTING** — Triggered just before packet egress. **Packet Traversal Order:** ``` NIC → PREROUTING (raw → mangle → nat DNAT) → Routing Decision ├──→ INPUT (mangle → filter → security) → Local Process ├──→ FORWARD (mangle → filter → security) → POSTROUTING └──→ OUTPUT (raw → mangle → nat → filter → security) → POSTROUTING POSTROUTING (mangle → nat SNAT) → NIC ``` For a complete labeled flowchart, refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes. --- **(b) — Configuration Script (7 marks):** ```bash # Step 1: Flush existing rules iptables -F iptables -t nat -F iptables -F INPUT iptables -F OUTPUT iptables -F FORWARD # Step 2: Set default policies iptables -P INPUT DROP iptables -P FORWARD DROP iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT # Step 3: Allow loopback iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT # Step 4: Allow established and related connections (stateful) iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT # Step 5: Allow SSH from LAN subnet only iptables -A INPUT -i eth1 -p tcp -s 192.168.10.0/24 --dport 22 \ -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT # Step 6: Allow HTTP and HTTPS from anywhere iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 80 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 443 -m state --state NEW -j ACCEPT # Step 7: Block external ping but allow LAN to ping iptables -A INPUT -i eth0 -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j DROP iptables -A INPUT -i eth1 -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT # Step 8: Enable IP forwarding echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward # Step 9: Configure MASQUERADE NAT iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE # Step 10: Allow LAN-to-WAN forwarding iptables -A FORWARD -i eth1 -o eth0 -s 192.168.10.0/24 \ -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT iptables -A FORWARD -i eth0 -o eth1 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT # Step 11: Verify and persist iptables -L -n -v --line-numbers netfilter-persistent save ``` --- ### Question B-B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **(a)** Compare iptables and pfSense as firewall solutions. Discuss their architectural differences, default rule behavior, and suitable deployment scenarios. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to configure pfSense as a perimeter firewall with WAN and LAN interfaces. List the essential default rules and explain how to add a custom rule to allow HTTPS traffic from the LAN to the WAN. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) — iptables vs pfSense Comparison (7 marks):** *(Refer to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3 for the full comparison table. The model answer should expand on these points:)* 1. **Platform & Engine:** iptables runs on Linux (netfilter kernel hooks); pfSense runs on FreeBSD using the PF (Packet Filter) engine. 2. **Interface:** iptables is purely command-line based; pfSense offers a feature-rich web GUI on HTTPS port 443. 3. **Default Rule:** iptables default is permissive (ACCEPT); pfSense default is restrictive (block WAN, allow LAN) — a security best practice out of the box. 4. **User-defined Chains:** iptables supports `-N` (new chain) for modular rule organization; pfSense uses **Aliases** and **Schedules** for the same purpose. 5. **High Availability:** iptables relies on external tools (Keepalived/VRRP); pfSense has **CARP** built-in. 6. **Deployment Scenarios:** iptables — best for Linux servers, cloud VMs, containers, Kubernetes nodes. pfSense — best for SMB perimeter, branch offices, dedicated firewall appliances. --- **(b) — pfSense Configuration Procedure (7 marks):** **Step 1: Installation** - Download pfSense ISO from `https://www.pfsense.org/download/`. - Install on a dedicated machine or virtual machine (VMware/VirtualBox/Proxmox) with **two NICs** (one WAN, one LAN). **Step 2: Initial Console Assignment** - At the console, assign interfaces: - `WAN` → `em0` (or first NIC) — gets IP via DHCP from upstream. - `LAN` → `em1` (or second NIC) — static IP `192.168.1.1/24`. - Default LAN admin URL: `https://192.168.1.1`. - Default credentials: `admin / pfsense`. **Step 3: Web Wizard Setup** - Login, complete the setup wizard (hostname, DNS, time zone, admin password). **Step 4: Default Rules (after install)** - **WAN:** Default block on all inbound traffic. - **LAN:** Default allow all to anywhere (Anti-Lockout rule on port 443 to the firewall itself is implicit). **Step 5: Add Custom Rule for LAN → WAN HTTPS** - Navigate to **Firewall → Rules → LAN**. - Click **Add** (up arrow to insert at top). - Configure: - **Action:** Pass - **Interface:** LAN - **Address Family:** IPv4 - **Protocol:** TCP - **Source:** LAN net - **Destination:** Any - **Destination Port:** 443 (HTTPS) - **Description:** "Allow LAN clients to reach HTTPS sites on WAN" - Click **Save** → **Apply Changes**. **Step 6: Verification** - From a LAN client, browse `https://www.google.com` — should succeed. - Check logs at **Status → System Logs → Firewall** to confirm the rule is being hit. **Step 7: Backup** - Go to **Diagnostics → Backup & Restore** → download the `config.xml`. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Rule Order Error (–2 marks):** Placing a broad `DROP` rule **above** a specific `ACCEPT` rule. The first-match-wins logic is forgotten. > 2. **Missing ESTABLISHED/RELATED Rule (–1.5 marks):** Without this, all return traffic is blocked and services appear to "hang" from the user's side. > 3. **Forgetting IP Forwarding (–1 mark):** Setting up NAT MASQUERADE without `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` — packets are silently dropped at routing. > 4. **Wrong Table Specification (–1 mark):** Writing `iptables -A POSTROUTING` (filter table) instead of `iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING`. The filter table has no POSTROUTING chain on some kernels. > 5. **Not Persisting Rules (–0.5 marks):** Rules are lost on reboot if not saved with `netfilter-persistent save` (Debian) or `service iptables save` (RHEL). > 6. **Confusing pfSense Default Behavior (–1 mark):** Stating pfSense allows all by default — the default is the opposite (WAN blocked, LAN allowed). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam:** - ☐ **Firewall** = security device that filters packets by rule. - ☐ **iptables** = Linux CLI tool; **netfilter** = underlying kernel framework. - ☐ **pfSense** = FreeBSD-based GUI firewall using **PF**. - ☐ **5 Tables:** filter, nat, mangle, raw, security. - ☐ **5 Chains:** PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING. - ☐ **Default Table:** filter. **Default Chains in filter:** INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT. - ☐ **Rule Format:** `iptables [-t table] -A chain match -j target`. - ☐ **Targets:** ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT, LOG, SNAT, DNAT, MASQUERADE, RETURN. - ☐ **DROP** = silent discard. **REJECT** = discard + ICMP error. - ☐ **SNAT** = static source IP rewrite. **MASQUERADE** = dynamic source IP rewrite. - ☐ **DNAT** = destination rewrite (used for port forwarding). - ☐ **Stateful matches:** NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID. - ☐ **First-match-wins** rule semantics — order matters critically. - ☐ **Always allow ESTABLISHED,RELATED** to permit return traffic. - ☐ **IP forwarding** must be enabled for routing/NAT: `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`. - ☐ **Common command:** `iptables -L -n -v --line-numbers` to list rules. - ☐ **Persistence:** `netfilter-persistent save` (Debian) or `/etc/iptables.save` then restore. - ☐ **pfSense default:** Block WAN, Allow LAN (opposite of iptables default). - ☐ **pfSense config file:** `/conf/config.xml`. **Admin URL:** `https://`. - ☐ **Rule types:** filter (allow/block), nat (port forward, 1:1, outbound). - ☐ **pfSense first-match-wins**, just like iptables. - ☐ **Real-world usage:** perimeter defense, server hardening, multi-tenant isolation, compliance. ---

topics.title: Implementation of VPN protocols (IPSec and SSL/TLS) for secure communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implementation of VPN protocols (IPSec and SSL/TLS) for secure communication ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Virtual Private Network (VPN):** A VPN is a secured overlay network constructed over a public, untrusted infrastructure (typically the Internet) using tunneling protocols, encryption, authentication, and integrity mechanisms to provide confidentiality, integrity, and endpoint authentication for data in transit between two communicating entities. For the scope of **PCITL607 – Network Security Lab (Module 2)**, students are required to **implement and demonstrate** two dominant VPN protocol families: 1. **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** — A network-layer (Layer 3) security framework defined by the IETF in **RFC 4301–RFC 4309**, designed to protect every packet flowing between two endpoints. 2. **SSL/TLS (Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security)** — A session-layer (Layer 4–5) security protocol defined in **RFC 5246 (TLS 1.2)** and **RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3)**, designed to secure application-layer sessions such as HTTPS, SMTPS, and FTPS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is **hands-on implementation** oriented. Students must (a) configure an **IPSec tunnel** between two Linux/Windows hosts using `strongSwan`, `Libreswan`, or `Openswan`, and (b) demonstrate an **SSL/TLS secured web session** using `OpenSSL` or `stunnel`. Viva questions typically target the **difference between the two protocols**, the **handshake steps**, and the **cryptographic primitives used**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Armored Tunnel" Imagine you want to send a **sealed, tamper-proof letter** through a city where strangers can read, copy, or modify any postcard. You have two options: - **Option A — IPSec (Network-Layer Tunnel):** You put your letter inside a **blast-proof armored truck** that drives on a dedicated highway lane. Even the truck driver cannot open the cargo bay. **Every vehicle on that highway is protected** — not just yours. This represents IPSec, which secures **all traffic** between two networks (site-to-site) or two hosts (host-to-host) at the IP layer. - **Option B — SSL/TLS (Application-Layer Tunnel):** You and your friend meet, agree on a **secret language and a codebook**, then exchange letters in a **locked briefcase with a unique key** for *this conversation only*. Other conversations in the building use different keys. This represents TLS, which secures **specific application sessions** (e.g., one HTTPS browser tab) at the transport layer. > [!TIP] > **One-line memory hook:** *IPSec protects the ROAD; TLS protects the CONVERSATION on the road.* ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Cryptographic Primitives in Use | Metric / Primitive | IPSec Default | SSL/TLS 1.2 Default | TLS 1.3 Default | |---|---|---|---| | **Symmetric Cipher** | AES-256 (CBC or GCM) | AES-128/256 (CBC or GCM) | AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 | | **Key Exchange** | IKEv2 (Diffie-Hellman) | RSA or (EC)DH | (EC)DH only | | **Hash / MAC** | HMAC-SHA-256 | HMAC-SHA-256 / SHA-384 | HKDF + Poly1305 | | **Authentication** | X.509 certificates or PSK | X.509 certificates | X.509 certificates (PSK optional) | | **Operating Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4–5 (Session/Presentation) | Layer 4–5 | | **Default Port** | N/A (uses IP proto 50, 51) | 443 (HTTPS), 993, 995, etc. | 443 | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Viva Trap:** TLS does NOT operate at the *Application Layer*. It sits between TCP and the application, often classified at **Layer 5 (Session)** in the OSI model. Stating "TLS is a Layer 7 protocol" will cost marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 IPSec Architecture — The Three Pillars IPSec is **not a single protocol** but a **suite** of three interacting components: 1. **AH (Authentication Header) — RFC 4302** - Provides **integrity + authentication** of the IP payload and most of the IP header. - Uses **HMAC** (Hash-based Message Authentication Code). - Does **NOT provide confidentiality** (no encryption). - IP Protocol Number: **51**. 2. **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) — RFC 4303** - Provides **integrity + authentication + confidentiality** (encryption). - Most widely deployed IPSec protocol. - IP Protocol Number: **50**. 3. **IKE (Internet Key Exchange) — RFC 7296 (IKEv2)** - A **key management protocol** that negotiates Security Associations (SAs), authenticates peers, and generates shared session keys using **Diffie-Hellman**. - Operates on UDP port **500** (or **4500** with NAT traversal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Security Association (SA):** A unidirectional logical contract between two peers that contains the agreed-upon cryptographic algorithms, keys, SPI (Security Parameters Index), and lifetime. ESP requires **two SAs** (one in each direction); AH also requires two. They are stored in a database called the **SAD (Security Association Database)**. ### 2.2 IPSec Modes of Operation | Mode | Scope | Encapsulation Overhead | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Transport Mode** | Secures only the **payload** of the original IP packet. Original IP header is preserved. | Low (≈ 30–57 bytes) | Host-to-host communication on a private network | | **Tunnel Mode** | The **entire original IP packet** is encrypted and encapsulated inside a new IP packet with a new outer header. | High (≈ 50–70 bytes) | Site-to-site VPN, remote-access VPN across the Internet | **Mathematical Representation of Encapsulation:** $$ \text{Tunnel\_Packet} = \underbrace{\text{New\_IP\_Header}}_{\text{outer}} + \underbrace{\text{ESP\_Header} + \text{Encrypted}\big(\text{Orig\_IP\_Header} + \text{Payload} + \text{ESP\_Trailer}\big) + \text{ESP\_Auth}}_{\text{ESP payload}} $$ ### 2.3 SSL/TLS Architecture — The Two-Layer Stack TLS 1.2 is organized into **two sub-protocols**: - **TLS Handshake Protocol** — Negotiates cipher suite, authenticates server (and optionally client), and generates shared keys. - **TLS Record Protocol** — Fragments, compresses (optional), encrypts, and MACs application data. **TLS 1.3 Simplification:** TLS 1.3 reduced the handshake from **2-RTT to 1-RTT** (or **0-RTT** for resumption) and removed static RSA key exchange. ### 2.4 The IKEv2 Handshake (Two Message Pairs) IKEv2 uses **four messages** to establish an IPSec SA: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Phase 1 (IKE\_SA\_INIT):} \quad & M_1: \text{Initiator} \rightarrow \text{Responder}: \{ \text{SA}_i, \text{KE}_i, \text{N}_i \} \\ & M_2: \text{Responder} \rightarrow \text{Initiator}: \{ \text{SA}_r, \text{KE}_r, \text{N}_r \} \\ \text{Phase 2 (IKE\_AUTH):} \quad & M_3: \text{Initiator} \rightarrow \text{Responder}: \{ \text{ID}_i, \text{AUTH}, \text{SA}_i, \text{TS}_i \} \\ & M_4: \text{Responder} \rightarrow \text{Initiator}: \{ \text{ID}_r, \text{AUTH}, \text{SA}_r, \text{TS}_r \} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - **KE** = Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange payload - **N** = Nonce (for freshness) - **AUTH** = Authentication data (digital signature or MAC) - **TS** = Traffic Selector (defines which IP subnets/hosts are protected) ### 2.5 The TLS 1.2 Handshake (Step-by-Step) $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{Step 1: Client} \rightarrow \text{Server: ClientHello (version, random, cipher suites, extensions)} \\ & \text{Step 2: Server} \rightarrow \text{Client: ServerHello + Certificate + ServerHelloDone} \\ & \text{Step 3: Client} \rightarrow \text{Server: ClientKeyExchange + ChangeCipherSpec + Finished} \\ & \text{Step 4: Server} \rightarrow \text{Client: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished} \\ & \text{Step 5: Encrypted Application Data exchange begins} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Standard | |---|---|---| | $K_{AB}$ | Shared secret between A and B | Result of (EC)DH | | $g^a \bmod p$, $g^b \bmod p$ | Diffie-Hellman public values | RFC 3526 groups | | $S$ | Shared DH secret $= g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Computed independently by both parties | | $\text{SPI}$ | Security Parameters Index | 32-bit identifier | | $\text{SAD}$ | Security Association Database | Kernel-resident (e.g., `/proc/net/ipsec_sadb`) | | $\text{SPD}$ | Security Policy Database | Defines PROTECT / BYPASS / DISCARD | | $H(M)$ | Hash of message $M$ | SHA-256, SHA-384 | | $\text{HMAC}_K(M)$ | Keyed hash | $\text{SHA-256}(K \oplus \text{opad} \,\vert\, \text{SHA-256}(K \oplus \text{ipad} \,\vert\, M))$ | | $E_K(P)$ | Symmetric encryption | AES-256-GCM | | $\text{AH\_ICV}$ | Integrity Check Value | Truncated HMAC, typically 96 bits | | $\text{MTU}_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective MTU after ESP overhead | $1500 - 73 = 1427$ bytes (typical) | | $R$ | TLS Client/Server Random | 32 bytes of strong randomness | | $\text{PRF}$ | Pseudo-Random Function (TLS 1.2) | P_SHA-256, P_SHA-384 | | $\text{HKDF}$ | HMAC-based KDF (TLS 1.3) | RFC 5869 | > [!TIP] > **MTU Tip:** If you forget to reduce the MTU on the VPN interface, large pings will fragment or drop silently. Always set `mtu=1400` and `mss=1360` in tunnel mode. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement (Used in BOTH IKEv2 and TLS) The DH algorithm solves the problem: *How do two parties agree on a secret over an insecure channel?* **Step 1 — Public Parameters:** Both parties agree on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ (where $1 < g < p$). These values are public and may be transmitted in the clear. **Step 2 — Private Key Generation:** Each party selects a secret random integer. Alice picks $a$ such that $2 \le a \le p-2$. Bob picks $b$ such that $2 \le b \le p-2$. **Step 3 — Public Value Computation:** Each party computes a public value. $$ A = g^{a} \bmod p $$ $$ B = g^{b} \bmod p $$ **Step 4 — Exchange:** Alice sends $A$ to Bob; Bob sends $B$ to Alice. An eavesdropper sees only $A$, $B$, $p$, and $g$. **Step 5 — Shared Secret Computation:** Each party combines their private key with the other's public value. $$ S_{\text{Alice}} = B^{a} \bmod p = (g^{b})^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ $$ S_{\text{Bob}} = A^{b} \bmod p = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ Therefore: $$ S_{\text{Alice}} = S_{\text{Bob}} = S = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ **Step 6 — Why is this secure?** The Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) — given $A$, $g$, and $p$, computing $a$ — is computationally infeasible for primes $\ge 2048$ bits. This is called the **Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH) assumption**. > [!NOTE] > **Worked numeric example (small $p$ for teaching):** Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$. Alice picks $a = 6$: $A = 5^6 \bmod 23 = 15625 \bmod 23 = 8$. Bob picks $b = 15$: $B = 5^{15} \bmod 23 = 8$ (coincidence). Both compute $S = 8^6 \bmod 23 = 8^{15} \bmod 23 = 13$. Shared secret = **13**. ### 3.2 Derivation: IPSec ESP Packet Size in Tunnel Mode Given an **original IPv4 packet** of size $L_o$ bytes and ESP with AES-CBC (block size 16) and HMAC-SHA-256-128: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Encrypted Payload} &= \text{Orig\_IP\_Header} + \text{Orig\_Payload} + \text{ESP\_Trailer} + \text{Padding} \\ \text{ESP\_Trailer} &= \text{Pad Length (1B)} + \text{Next Header (1B)} = 2 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Block-aligned size} &= \lceil (L_o - 20 + 2) / 16 \rceil \times 16 \\ \text{ESP\_Auth (ICV)} &= 16 \text{ bytes (truncated HMAC-SHA-256)} \\ \text{New\_IP\_Header} &= 20 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{ESP\_Header (SPI + Seq\#)} &= 8 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Total} &= 20 + 8 + \text{Block-aligned size} + 16 = 44 + \text{Block-aligned size} \end{aligned} $$ **For a typical 1500-byte payload ($L_o = 1500$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Block-aligned size} &= \lceil (1480 + 2) / 16 \rceil \times 16 = \lceil 1482/16 \rceil \times 16 = 93 \times 16 = 1488 \\ \text{Total ESP packet} &= 44 + 1488 = 1532 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ This **exceeds the standard 1500-byte MTU**, which is why **Path MTU Discovery** or manual MTU clamping is mandatory in IPSec tunnel deployments. ### 3.3 Derivation: TLS 1.2 Master Secret Computation After the handshake, the client and server share three random values: - $R_C$ = Client Random (32 bytes) - $R_S$ = Server Random (32 bytes) - $\text{PMS}$ = Pre-Master Secret (48 bytes for RSA; variable for DH) The **Master Secret (MS)** is computed as: $$ \text{MS} = \text{PRF}_{48}\!\left( \text{PMS},\ \text{"master secret"},\ R_C \,\Vert\, R_S \right) $$ The PRF (Pseudo-Random Function) for TLS 1.2 with SHA-256 expands the secret as: $$ \text{PRF}_{n}(\text{secret}, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = P_{\text{HSHA256}}(\text{secret}, \text{label} + \text{seed})[0 \ldots n-1] $$ Where $P_{\text{HSHA256}}$ is defined in **RFC 5246 Section 5** as the iterative HMAC-SHA-256 expansion: $$ \begin{aligned} A(0) &= \text{HMAC}(\text{secret}, \text{label} + \text{seed}) \\ A(i) &= \text{HMAC}(\text{secret}, A(i-1)) \quad \text{for } i \ge 1 \\ P(\text{secret}, \text{label}, \text{seed}) &= A(1) + A(2) + A(3) + \cdots \end{aligned} $$ From the Master Secret, **key block** is derived and split into: $$ \text{KeyBlock} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS}, \text{"key expansion"},\ R_S \,\Vert\, R_C) $$ $$ \text{KeyBlock} \rightarrow \big[ \text{client\_MAC\_key} \,\vert\, \text{server\_MAC\_key} \,\vert\, \text{client\_enc\_key} \,\vert\, \text{server\_enc\_key} \,\vert\, \text{client\_IV} \,\vert\, \text{server\_IV} \big] $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: IPSec vs. SSL/TLS (High-Yield Comparison) | Parameter | IPSec | SSL/TLS | |---|---|---| | **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4–5 (Transport/Session) | | **Scope of Protection** | All IP traffic between endpoints | Single application session | | **Granularity** | Per-packet, per-flow, per-host | Per-connection (one session) | | **Authentication** | X.509 / PSK | X.509 (PSK in TLS 1.3) | | **Key Exchange** | IKEv2 (DH/ECDH) | (EC)DH (RSA legacy) | | **Encryption** | ESP (AES, 3DES, ChaCha20) | AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 | | **NAT Traversal** | Encapsulated in UDP 4500 (NAT-T) | Works naturally over TCP/UDP | | **User Awareness** | Transparent (no per-app config) | Visible (browser padlock, cert warnings) | | **Configuration** | OS-level / firewall | Application / library level | | **Typical Use** | Site-to-site gateway VPN, remote access | HTTPS, secure email, API security | | **Firewall Friendliness** | Poor (often blocked) | Excellent (uses port 443) | | **Header Overhead** | 50–73 bytes (tunnel) | 20–40 bytes (record) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: IPSec Site-to-Site VPN (Two Linux Hosts with strongSwan) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HOST_A["HOST A (Left Gateway) - 192.168.10.1"] APP_A["Application Traffic
10.10.10.0/24"] KERNEL_A["Linux Kernel
IPSec Stack"] STRONGSWAN_A["strongSwan Daemon
charon"] CERT_A["Local Certificate
/etc/ipsec.d/certs/hostA.crt"] CONFIG_A["ipsec.conf
conn myvpn"] end subgraph INTERNET["PUBLIC INTERNET (Untrusted)"] ESP_PKT["ESP Packet
UDP 4500 / Proto 50"] end subgraph HOST_B["HOST B (Right Gateway) - 192.168.20.1"] APP_B["Application Traffic
10.10.20.0/24"] KERNEL_B["Linux Kernel
IPSec Stack"] STRONGSWAN_B["strongSwan Daemon
charon"] CERT_B["Local Certificate
/etc/ipsec.d/certs/hostB.crt"] CONFIG_B["ipsec.conf
conn myvpn"] end APP_A --> CONFIG_A CONFIG_A --> STRONGSWAN_A STRONGSWAN_A <--> CERT_A STRONGSWAN_A --> KERNEL_A KERNEL_A --> ESP_PKT ESP_PKT --> KERNEL_B KERNEL_B --> STRONGSWAN_B STRONGSWAN_B <--> CERT_B STRONGSWAN_B --> APP_B ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: IKEv2 Handshake (4 Messages) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant I as Initiator (Host A) participant R as Responder (Host B) Note over I,R: IKE_SA_INIT Exchange (UDP 500) I->>R: M1: SAi, KEi, Ni R->>I: M2: SAr, KEr, Nr Note over I,R: Both compute S = g^ab mod p Note over I,R: IKE_AUTH Exchange I->>R: M3: IDi, AUTH, SAp2i, TSi R->>I: M4: IDr, AUTH, SAp2r, TSr Note over I,R: IPSec SA established (ESP Tunnel UP) I->>R: ESP Encrypted Data (proto 50) R->>I: ESP Encrypted Data (proto 50) ``` ### 4.3 Sequence Diagram: TLS 1.2 Handshake (Full) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client (Browser) participant S as Server (Web) C->>S: ClientHello
version, R_C, cipher_suites S->>C: ServerHello
version, R_S, chosen_cipher S->>C: Certificate (X.509) S->>C: ServerHelloDone Note over C: Verify cert, generate PMS C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (encrypted PMS or DH public) C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec C->>S: Finished (verify_data) Note over C,S: Derive Master Secret + Keys S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec S->>C: Finished (verify_data) Note over C,S: Application Data (encrypted) C->>S: GET /index.html (TLS record) S->>C: 200 OK (TLS record) ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: SSL/TLS Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Application Layer"] HTTP["HTTP / SMTP / FTP / LDAP"] end subgraph TLS["TLS Protocol (Layer 5)"] HANDSHAKE["Handshake Protocol"] ALERT["Alert Protocol"] CHANGECIPHER["Change Cipher Spec"] APPDATA["Application Data Protocol"] end subgraph RECORD["TLS Record Protocol"] FRAG["Fragmentation"] COMPRESS["Compression (optional)"] MAC["MAC Computation"] ENCRYPT["Encryption (AES-GCM)"] HDR["Record Header (5 bytes)"] end subgraph TRANS["Transport Layer"] TCP["TCP (port 443)"] end subgraph NET["Network Layer"] IP["IP (IPv4 / IPv6)"] end HTTP --> HANDSHAKE HTTP --> APPDATA HANDSHAKE --> FRAG ALERT --> FRAG CHANGECIPHER --> FRAG APPDATA --> FRAG FRAG --> COMPRESS --> MAC --> ENCRYPT --> HDR --> TCP --> IP ``` ### 4.5 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Between IPSec and SSL/TLS ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Need a VPN?"] --> Q1{"What do you need
to protect?"} Q1 -- "Entire network between
two offices" --> IPSEC1["Use IPSec
Site-to-Site Tunnel Mode"] Q1 -- "A specific application
session (web, mail)" --> TLS1["Use SSL/TLS
Reverse Proxy or stunnel"] Q1 -- "Remote users connecting
to corporate network" --> Q2{"Granularity needed?"} Q2 -- "Full network access" --> IPSEC2["Use IPSec
Remote Access (IKEv2)"] Q2 -- "Per-application access" --> TLS2["Use SSL/TLS
Clientless VPN (HTTPS portal)"] IPSEC1 --> ENDOK["Configuration done"] IPSEC2 --> ENDOK TLS1 --> ENDOK TLS2 --> ENDOK ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between IPSec Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode. In which scenario would you prefer Tunnel Mode for a site-to-site VPN?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Transport Mode (1 mark):** Encrypts/ authenticates only the **payload** of the IP packet. The original IP header remains intact. Used mainly for **host-to-host** communication on a private network. Low overhead (≈ 30 bytes). - **Tunnel Mode (1 mark):** Encapsulates the **entire original IP packet** (header + payload) inside a new IP packet with a new outer header. Used for **site-to-site VPN** and **remote-access VPN** across the Internet. High overhead (≈ 50–70 bytes). - **Site-to-site justification (1 mark):** In a site-to-site VPN, the two gateway routers use **public IP addresses** as the outer header to route the packet across the Internet, while the **inner IP header carries the private subnet addresses** of the original sender/receiver. Only Tunnel Mode can hide the private IP addressing scheme of the internal networks, making it the correct choice. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three differences between SSL/TLS and IPSec. State the default port number for HTTPS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **OSI Layer:** SSL/TLS operates at Layer 5 (Session), while IPSec operates at Layer 3 (Network). *(1 mark)* 2. **Scope:** SSL/TLS protects a single application session (e.g., one HTTPS connection), while IPSec protects **all IP traffic** between endpoints. *(1 mark)* 3. **Configuration:** SSL/TLS is configured inside the application or library (e.g., Apache, OpenSSL), while IPSec is configured at the OS / kernel level and is transparent to applications. *(1 mark)* - **Default HTTPS port:** **443** *(included for completeness)*. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Module Choice Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** full question from the choice. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — IPSec-Focused** **Q.A (a) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set A]** **(7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the **IPSec ESP Header format in Tunnel Mode**. List the cryptographic services provided by ESP and AH, and identify the IP protocol numbers used. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **ESP Header Layout in Tunnel Mode (new outer IP header + ESP):** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{New IP Hdr (20B)} & \text{ESP Hdr (8B)} & \text{Orig IP Hdr} & \text{Original Payload} & \text{ESP Trailer} & \text{ESP Auth (ICV)} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ | Field | Size | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | 32 bits | Identifies the SA together with Dest IP + ESP | | **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter (monotonically increasing) | | **IV (Initialization Vector)** | 8–16 bytes (if CBC) | Random value for AES-CBC | | **Original IP Header + Payload** | variable | The encrypted inner packet (in Tunnel Mode) | | **Padding** | 0–255 bytes | Aligns to cipher block size (16 for AES) | | **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Number of pad bytes | | **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload (e.g., 4 = IPv4, 41 = IPv6) | | **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | 96–128 bits | HMAC-SHA-256 truncated output | **Services Provided (2 marks):** - **AH:** Integrity + Authentication + Anti-replay. **No** confidentiality. IP Proto **51**. - **ESP:** Integrity + Authentication + **Confidentiality** (encryption) + Anti-replay. IP Proto **50**. **Diagrammatic credit (1 mark).** Working explanation of how SPI, Seq#, and ICV are populated. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "AH provides encryption" — this is **wrong** and will cost 1–2 marks. AH is integrity-only.
