Also, students forget that ESP's ICV is computed over **ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer** (excluding the new IP header in Tunnel Mode). --- **Q.A (b) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set A]** **(7 marks)** Explain the **IKEv2 four-message handshake** with a neat sequence diagram. What is the role of the **Nonce** and the **Traffic Selector (TS)** in the exchange? **Model Answer (7 marks):** **IKEv2 Handshake (3 marks — diagram + description):** The exchange proceeds in two phases: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{IKE\_SA\_INIT Phase} & \\ \text{M1: Initiator} &\rightarrow \text{Responder: } SA_i, KE_i, N_i \\ \text{M2: Responder} &\rightarrow \text{Initiator: } SA_r, KE_r, N_r \\ \\ \textbf{IKE\_AUTH Phase} & \\ \text{M3: Initiator} &\rightarrow \text{Responder: } ID_i, AUTH_i, SA_{i2}, TS_i \\ \text{M4: Responder} &\rightarrow \text{Initiator: } ID_r, AUTH_r, SA_{r2}, TS_r \end{aligned} $$ - $SA_i, SA_r$ — cryptographic proposals - $KE_i, KE_r$ — Diffie-Hellman public values - $N_i, N_r$ — nonces (32-bit random, protected) - $ID_i, ID_r$ — peer identities (e.g., IP or email) - $AUTH$ — digital signature or HMAC proving identity - $TS$ — Traffic Selectors **Role of Nonce (2 marks):** Nonces $N_i$ and $N_r$ are random 32-bit (or longer) values included in the key derivation. They guarantee **freshness**, preventing replay attacks. The session keys are derived as: $$ \text{SKEYSEED} = \text{PRF}(N_i \,\Vert\, N_r,\ g^{ir}) $$ where $g^{ir} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ is the Diffie-Hellman shared secret. **Role of Traffic Selector (2 marks):** The TS payload specifies **which IP packets** are protected by the negotiated SA. It typically contains source and destination IP address ranges, port ranges, and protocol numbers. For example, `TS_i = 10.10.10.0/24 ↔ 10.10.20.0/24` defines that all traffic between these two subnets is protected, allowing fine-grained **granular policy enforcement**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Nonce prevents the attack" without specifying *which* attack. Be precise: nonces provide **replay protection** during key derivation. Also, do not confuse the IKEv2 **SKEYSEED** with the final **encryption key** — SKEYSEED is then expanded via PRF into multiple sub-keys (`SK_d, SK_ai, SK_ar, SK_ei, SK_er, SK_pi, SK_pr`). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — SSL/TLS-Focused** **Q.B (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]** **(7 marks)** Explain the **TLS 1.2 Handshake Protocol** in detail with a sequence diagram. Mention the role of the **Certificate**, **ClientKeyExchange**, and **Finished** messages. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step-by-step TLS 1.2 Handshake (4 marks):** | Step | Message | Direction | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **ClientHello** | C → S | Advertises TLS version, Client Random $R_C$, and list of cipher suites | | 2 | **ServerHello** | S → C | Selects version, Server Random $R_S$, chosen cipher suite | | 3 | **Certificate** | S → C | Server sends its X.509 certificate chain | | 4 | **ServerHelloDone** | S → C | Indicates server has finished its hello messages | | 5 | **ClientKeyExchange** | C → S | Client sends the Pre-Master Secret (PMS) — encrypted with server's public key (RSA) or as a DH public value | | 6 | **ChangeCipherSpec** | C → S | Indicates that subsequent records will use the newly negotiated cipher | | 7 | **Finished** | C → S | Encrypted hash of all previous handshake messages (verify_data) | | 8 | **ChangeCipherSpec** | S → C | Server switches to encrypted mode | | 9 | **Finished** | S → C | Encrypted hash of all handshake messages | **Role of Certificate (1 mark):** Provides the server's **public key** and its **identity binding** (signed by a trusted CA). The client verifies the chain up to a trusted root. Optionally, the server requests a client certificate for **mutual TLS (mTLS)**. **Role of ClientKeyExchange (1 mark):** Enables the client to securely transmit the **Pre-Master Secret (PMS)** to the server. With RSA, the PMS is encrypted with the server's public key; with (EC)DH, this message contains the client's DH public value. Both parties then compute the **Master Secret** as $\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS}, \text{"master secret"}, R_C \,\Vert\, R_S)$. **Role of Finished (1 mark):** The first message protected with the **newly negotiated keys**. It contains a `verify_data` = PRF of the Master Secret over the hash of all previous handshake messages. This proves that both parties derived the **same keys** and detects any MITM tampering. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often say "Certificate encrypts the Pre-Master Secret." Correction: only the **client's exchange of the PMS uses the certificate's public key** in RSA mode; the certificate itself is a *signed binding* between identity and key. Also note — **ChangeCipherSpec is NOT a handshake message** in TLS 1.3; it was removed. --- **Q.B (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]** **(7 marks)** Demonstrate the steps to **create a self-signed X.509 certificate** using `OpenSSL` and configure an **Apache HTTPS server** to use TLS. Show the commands and explain each step. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Generate the private key (1 mark):** ```bash openssl genrsa -out server.key 2048 ``` This creates a **2048-bit RSA private key** stored in PEM format. The key must be kept secret. **Step 2 — Generate a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) (1 mark):** ```bash openssl req -new -key server.key -out server.csr \ -subj "/C=IN/ST=Kerala/L=Kochi/O=KTU/CN=vpn.lab.local" ``` The CSR contains the public key and the **Distinguished Name (DN)** of the subject. The Common Name (CN) must match the hostname the client will use. **Step 3 — Create a self-signed certificate (valid for 365 days) (1 mark):** ```bash openssl x509 -req -days 365 -in server.csr \ -signkey server.key -out server.crt ``` This signs the CSR with its own private key (self-signing). In production, this step would use a CA's private key. **Step 4 — Enable the SSL module in Apache (1 mark):** ```bash sudo a2enmod ssl sudo a2ensite default-ssl ``` **Step 5 — Configure the Apache virtual host (2 marks):** Edit `/etc/apache2/sites-available/default-ssl.conf`: ```apache ServerName vpn.lab.local DocumentRoot /var/www/html SSLEngine on SSLCertificateFile /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/ssl/private/server.key SSLCipherSuite HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5 SSLProtocol all -SSLv3 -TLSv1 -TLSv1.1 ``` **Step 6 — Restart and test (1 mark):** ```bash sudo systemctl restart apache2 openssl s_client -connect vpn.lab.local:443 -showcerts ``` The `s_client` command displays the **server certificate chain**, negotiated cipher suite, and verifies the TLS handshake. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to enable the `ssl` and `headers` modules. They also fail to mention that the private key (`server.key`) must have **chmod 600** permissions. Finally, **self-signed certificates** will produce browser warnings; the explanation of *why* (not in the trusted CA store) is often expected. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Causes in PCITL607 Module 2:** > 1. **Confusing AH and ESP:** AH = integrity only (proto 51); ESP = integrity + encryption (proto 50). Mixing these up = −1 to −2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting Diffie-Hellman in the explanation:** Both IKEv2 and TLS use (EC)DH for **forward secrecy**. Stating "RSA encrypts the session key" is only true for legacy TLS 1.2 with RSA key exchange. > 3. **Skipping the Finished message in TLS:** This message is the **only proof that both parties derived matching keys**. > 4. **Not specifying Transport vs. Tunnel Mode:** A generic "IPSec encrypts data" answer loses 1 mark. > 5. **Writing the port number as "port 80" for HTTPS:** HTTPS = **443**, not 80. This is a basic 1-mark deduction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision List — Memorize Before Exam Day:** - **VPN** = secured overlay over an untrusted public network, using tunneling + crypto. - **IPSec** = Layer 3 framework, transparent to applications, site-to-site friendly. - **SSL/TLS** = Layer 4–5 protocol, per-application, runs on TCP/UDP ports (e.g., 443). - **AH = proto 51, integrity + auth only, no encryption.** Avoid in modern deployments. - **ESP = proto 50, integrity + auth + encryption.** Default for production VPNs. - **IKEv2** = UDP 500 (or 4500 with NAT-T), 4 messages total, uses (EC)DH for PFS. - **Security Association (SA)** = unidirectional contract; ESP needs **2 SAs** (one each way). - **Transport Mode** = original IP header kept; **Tunnel Mode** = new outer IP header. - **TLS 1.2 Handshake** = 2-RTT, 9 messages, ends with encrypted `Finished`. - **TLS 1.3** = 1-RTT, no RSA key exchange, no ChangeCipherSpec, mandatory forward secrecy. - **Diffie-Hellman** = shared secret $= g^{ab} \bmod p$, security relies on DLP. - **Nonce** = ensures freshness, prevents replay during key derivation. - **Traffic Selector (TS)** = defines IP/protocol/port range protected by the SA. - **Master Secret in TLS 1.2** = `PRF(PMS, "master secret", R_C ‖ R_S)`. - **OpenSSL commands to remember:** `genrsa`, `req`, `x509`, `s_client`, `s_server`. - **MTU tip:** Clamp VPN tunnel MTU to **1400 bytes** to avoid silent fragmentation. - **HTTPS default port = 443.** SMTP-TLS = 587, IMAPS = 993, FTPS = 990. - **Self-signed certs** trigger browser warnings; production uses **CA-signed** certificates. - **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** = both client and server present certificates for **two-way authentication**. - **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** = compromised long-term key cannot decrypt past sessions (achieved by ephemeral DH). - **SAD** stores active SAs; **SPD** stores policies (PROTECT / BYPASS / DISCARD). - **stunnel** = tool to wrap any TCP service with TLS without modifying the app (useful in lab demos). - **strongSwan / Libreswan** = Linux IPSec implementations; managed via `ipsec.conf` and the `charon` daemon. > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCITL607 Network Security Lab**

topics.title: Testing and validation of cryptographic protocols (SSL/TLS) in securing web transactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL607 - NETWORK SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing and validation of cryptographic protocols (SSL/TLS) in securing web transactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SSL (Secure Sockets Layer)** and its successor **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** are cryptographic application-layer protocols that provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** for communications over an insecure network (typically the Internet). They operate as a security shim between the application (e.g., HTTP, SMTP, FTP) and the transport (TCP) layer.

According to the **IETF standards (RFC 6101 for SSLv3, RFC 5246 for TLS 1.2, RFC 8446 for TLS 1.3)**, TLS is the de-facto backbone of all secure web transactions prefixed with `https://`. The protocol achieves its security objectives through a combination of:
- **Asymmetric cryptography** (RSA, ECDSA, DH, ECDHE) for authentication and key exchange
- **Symmetric cryptography** (AES-128/256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) for bulk data encryption
- **Cryptographic hash functions** (SHA-256, SHA-384) for integrity and PRF (Pseudo-Random Function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *TLS is an application-layer-independent cryptographic protocol that establishes a secure channel between two communicating peers through a structured handshake involving authentication (X.509 certificates), key exchange (public-key algorithms), and bulk data encryption (symmetric ciphers) — providing E2EE (End-to-End Encryption) for HTTP transactions.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to send a **highly confidential business contract** to a client across the city, but your courier is untrustworthy. The **"Courier Problem"** has multiple solutions, mirroring TLS:

| Real-World Problem | TLS Solution |
|---|---|
| Lock the box, but how do you share the key? | **Asymmetric cryptography** (public/private key pair) |
| How do you know the box actually came from your client? | **X.509 Digital Certificates** (signed by trusted CA) |
| The courier tampers with the box contents | **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** ensures integrity |
| The box is too slow to lock/unlock for every item | **Session keys** (symmetric) for bulk data; asymmetric only for handshake |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Royal Seal" Analogy:** TLS is like a king sending a sealed scroll. The seal (digital signature via X.509 cert) proves authenticity, the wax (HMAC) detects tampering, and the locked scroll box (AES session key) keeps content private. The handshake is the meeting where both parties exchange keys under heavy guard.

### 1.2.1 The Three Pillars of TLS (CIA Triad + Authentication)

$$
\text{TLS Security} = \underbrace{\text{Confidentiality}}_{\text{AES/Symmetric}} + \underbrace{\text{Integrity}}_{\text{HMAC/SHA}} + \underbrace{\text{Authentication}}_{\text{X.509/RSA/ECDSA}}
$$

---

## 1.3 Why SSL/TLS is Critical for Web Transactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Without TLS, HTTP traffic travels as **plaintext** — meaning any attacker on the same network (coffee shop Wi-Fi, ISP) can read passwords, cookies, and credit card numbers using tools like **Wireshark**. TLS prevents this by encrypting the entire HTTP payload.

### 1.3.1 Standard Metrics Used in TLS Evaluation

- **Latency Overhead:** **2 extra RTTs** in TLS 1.2, **1 RTT** in TLS 1.3, **0 RTT** in TLS 1.3 resumption mode
- **Cipher Suite Strength:** Modern acceptable key size ≥ **128 bits** for symmetric, ≥ **2048 bits** for RSA, ≥ **256 bits** for ECC
- **Session Resumption Time:** **0–100 ms** (via Session Ticket or Session ID)
- **Handshake Message Size:** typically **2 KB – 5 KB** before payload

---

## 1.4 OSI/TCP-IP Layer Placement

| Layer | Protocol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 7 (Application) | HTTP, SMTP, FTP | Original data |
| 6/5 (Security Shim) | **TLS Record / TLS Handshake** | **Encryption + Authentication** |
| 4 (Transport) | TCP | Reliable delivery |
| 3 (Network) | IP | Routing |
| 2/1 (Data Link/Physical) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | Physical transmission |

The TLS record layer sits **above TCP (port 443 by default)** but **below the application protocol** — a position that makes it transparent to the application developer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TLS Protocol Stack (Sub-Protocols)

TLS is divided into **four tightly coupled sub-protocols**, each with a specific purpose:

1. **TLS Handshake Protocol** – Negotiates algorithms, authenticates server (optionally client), establishes shared secrets.
2. **TLS Change Cipher Spec Protocol** – Signals transition to newly negotiated parameters.
3. **TLS Alert Protocol** – Conveys errors, warnings, and closure notices.
4. **TLS Record Protocol** – Fragments, compresses (optional), encrypts, and MACs application data.

### 2.1.1 Detailed Layered View

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Application Data (HTTP, SMTP, etc.)             |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  TLS Handshake | Change Cipher Spec | Alert      |  ← Sub-protocols
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  TLS Record Protocol (fragment + encrypt + MAC)  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)             |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  IP (Internet Protocol)                          |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

---

## 2.2 TLS Handshake — Phase-by-Phase Logical Breakdown

The TLS 1.2 Full Handshake consists of **2 RTTs (Round-Trip Times)**. Each message carries a specific cryptographic purpose.

### Phase 1: Capability Negotiation (Client → Server)
- Client sends **ClientHello** containing:
  - Highest supported TLS version (e.g., `TLS 1.2`)
  - 32-byte random nonce `client_random`
  - Session ID (for resumption)
  - List of supported **cipher suites**
  - List of supported **compression methods**
  - Optional extensions (SNI, ALPN)

### Phase 2: Server Identity & Negotiation (Server → Client)
- Server replies with **ServerHello**:
  - Chosen TLS version
  - 32-byte random nonce `server_random`
  - Chosen cipher suite
  - Compression method (typically `null`)
- Followed by **Certificate** message (X.509 chain)
- Optional **ServerKeyExchange** (for DHE/ECDHE)
- **ServerHelloDone**

### Phase 3: Client Key Submission (Client → Server)
- **ClientKeyExchange** carries the **pre-master secret** (encrypted with server's public RSA key) **OR** the DH public value
- **CertificateVerify** (if mutual auth)
- **ChangeCipherSpec** + **Finished** (HMAC over all previous messages)

### Phase 4: Server Confirmation (Server → Client)
- **ChangeCipherSpec** + **Finished**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** The handshake produces three critical secret values — **pre-master secret → master secret → key block** — from which **4–6 separate keys** are derived (client/server write keys, MAC keys, IVs).

---

## 2.3 The Master Secret Derivation Chain

The single most important cryptographic process in TLS is deriving all session keys from a single shared secret.

### Step 1: Pre-Master Secret (PMS)
- For **RSA key transport:** PMS is a 48-byte random value, encrypted with the server's public key and sent in ClientKeyExchange.
- For **(EC)DHE key agreement:** PMS is the shared Diffie-Hellman value `g^ab mod p`.

### Step 2: Master Secret (MS) Computation

The master secret is computed using the **TLS Pseudo-Random Function (PRF)**, which is a **HMAC-based KDF (Key Derivation Function)**.

$$
\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(\text{pre\_master\_secret},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{client\_random} \;\|\; \text{server\_random})
$$

The PRF is defined as:

$$
\text{PRF}(\text{secret},\; \text{label},\; \text{seed}) = \text{P\_hash}(\text{secret},\; \text{label} + \text{seed})
$$

Where `P_hash` expands a secret to an arbitrary number of bytes:

$$
\text{P\_hash}(\text{secret},\; \text{seed}) = \text{HMAC}(K=\text{secret}, m=A(1) + \text{seed}) \;\|\; \text{HMAC}(K=\text{secret}, m=A(2) + \text{seed}) \;\|\; \cdots
$$

With:

$$
A(0) = \text{seed}, \quad A(i) = \text{HMAC}(K=\text{secret}, m=A(i-1))
$$

For **TLS 1.2**, the PRF is explicitly defined using **SHA-256** (or SHA-384 for SHA-384 cipher suites):

$$
\text{PRF}_{\text{TLS12}}(\text{secret},\; \text{label},\; \text{seed}) = \text{P\_SHA256}(\text{secret},\; \text{label} + \text{seed})
$$

### Step 3: Key Block Generation

$$
\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{master\_secret},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{server\_random} \;\|\; \text{client\_random})
$$

The key block is then partitioned into:

$$
\text{key\_block} = \underbrace{c\_\text{MAC}}_{\text{client MAC}} \;\|\; \underbrace{s\_\text{MAC}}_{\text{server MAC}} \;\|\; \underbrace{c\_\text{key}}_{\text{client enc}} \;\|\; \underbrace{s\_\text{key}}_{\text{server enc}} \;\|\; \underbrace{c\_\text{IV}}_{\text{client IV}} \;\|\; \underbrace{s\_\text{IV}}_{\text{server IV}}
$$

The actual length of each segment depends on the negotiated **cipher suite** parameters.

---

## 2.4 TLS Record Protocol — Encryption & Integrity in Action

Once the handshake completes, every application data record goes through this pipeline:

```
Application Data
       ↓
[Fragmentation: max 16 KB per record]
       ↓
[Compression: optional, usually null]
       ↓
[MAC Computation: HMAC-MD5 / HMAC-SHA256]
       ↓
[Encryption: AES-CBC / AES-GCM / ChaCha20]
       ↓
[TLS Record Header added: type, version, length]
       ↓
TCP segment → IP packet
```

### 2.4.1 MAC-then-Encrypt vs Encrypt-then-MAC (CBC vs AEAD)

| Mode | TLS Version | Security Note |
|---|---|---|
| **MAC-then-Encrypt** | TLS 1.0 – 1.2 (CBC suites) | Vulnerable to **Lucky 13**, **POODLE** |
| **AEAD (Encrypt + MAC fused)** | TLS 1.2 (GCM suites), TLS 1.3 (mandatory) | **Recommended.** Galois/Counter Mode provides authenticated encryption |

For **AES-128-GCM**, the record encryption is:

$$
C = E_{k}(\text{Plaintext}) \quad \text{with} \quad T = \text{GHASH}(H, A, C)
$$

Where:
- $H = E_k(0^{128})$ (auth hash key)
- $A$ = additional authenticated data (record header)
- $C$ = ciphertext
- $T$ = 128-bit authentication tag

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** per RFC 5246, RFC 8446, and NIST SP 800-108.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{client\_random} \,\|\, \text{server\_random})$ | Master secret derivation (TLS 1.2) | RFC 5246 §8.1 |
| 2 | $\text{PRF}(S, L, \text{seed}) = P\_\text{hash}(S, L + \text{seed})$ | TLS Pseudo-Random Function | RFC 5246 §5 |
| 3 | $A(0) = \text{seed},\; A(i) = \text{HMAC}(S, A(i-1))$ | Iterative HMAC chain in P_hash | RFC 5246 §5 |
| 4 | $\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{S\_rand} \,\|\, \text{C\_rand})$ | Session key block generation | RFC 5246 §6.3 |
| 5 | $T = \text{GHASH}(H, A, C) \oplus E_k(J_0)$ | AES-GCM authentication tag | NIST SP 800-38D |
| 6 | $\text{DH shared} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Diffie-Hellman shared secret | RFC 2631 |
| 7 | $\text{ECDH shared} = d_A \cdot Q_B = d_B \cdot Q_A$ | Elliptic Curve DH | RFC 4492 |
| 8 | $\text{Finished} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"client finished"},\; \text{Hash}(\text{all msgs}))$ | Handshake authentication | RFC 5246 §7.4.9 |
| 9 | $\text{verify\_data} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"server finished"},\; \text{Hash}(\text{handshake}))$ | Server Finished MAC | RFC 5246 |
| 10 | $\text{CertChain} = \{\text{Leaf}, \text{Inter}, \text{Root}\}$ | X.509 chain-of-trust | RFC 5280 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **pre-master secret** with the **master secret**. The PMS is the *raw shared value* (e.g., DH result or RSA-encrypted random), while the MS is the *KDF output* of length exactly **48 bytes**. They are **not the same**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTTPS Everywhere:** Every `https://` URL uses TLS — this is the **single largest deployment of cryptography in human history**.
- **Email security:** SMTPS (port 465), IMAPS (port 993), POP3S (port 995) all rely on TLS.
- **VPNs:** OpenVPN uses TLS for control channel; WireGuard uses Noise_IK.
- **IoT/MQTT:** MQTT over TLS (port 8883) secures industrial control telemetry.
- **APIs:** REST/GraphQL APIs use TLS to prevent token/credential leakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Note:** In modern DevSecOps, **SSL Labs (ssllabs.com/ssltest)**, **testssl.sh**, and **nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers** are the standard tools used by **penetration testers** to validate TLS configurations on web servers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Pre-Master Secret to Encrypted Record

This section performs a **complete mathematical walk-through** of a TLS 1.2 session using **ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256** as the cipher suite. Every cryptographic transition is shown explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Assumed Inputs

- **Client random:** `client_random` (32 bytes)
- **Server random:** `server_random` (32 bytes)
- **Pre-master secret (PMS):** result of ECDH = `d_client · Q_server` (32 bytes for P-256)
- **Cipher suite:** `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Compute the Master Secret

$$
\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{client\_random} \;\|\; \text{server\_random})
$$

The PRF is the SHA-256-based expansion function:

$$
\text{PRF}(S, L, \text{seed}) = A(1) \,\|\, A(2) \,\|\, A(3)
$$

Where each $A(i)$ is 32 bytes of SHA-256 HMAC output. Since the master secret must be exactly **48 bytes**, we truncate to 48 bytes from the first iteration outputs.

**Step 1a:** Compute $A(1)$:
$$
A(0) = \text{"master secret"} + \text{client\_random} \,\|\, \text{server\_random}
$$
$$
A(1) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{PMS},\; m=A(0))
$$

**Step 1b:** Compute $A(2)$:
$$
A(2) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{PMS},\; m=A(1))
$$

**Step 1c:** Concatenate and truncate:
$$
\text{MS} = (A(1) \,\|\, A(2))[0 \text{ to } 47]
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The PRF is iterated until ≥ 48 bytes are produced. The 48-byte truncation guarantees deterministic output regardless of HMAC internal padding.

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Compute the Key Block

The key block must be long enough to hold all session keys. For `AES_128_GCM_SHA256`:

| Component | Length (bytes) |
|---|---|
| client_write_MAC_key | 0 (GCM uses no separate MAC) |
| server_write_MAC_key | 0 |
| client_write_key | 16 (AES-128) |
| server_write_key | 16 (AES-128) |
| client_write_IV | 4 (implicit salt, 8 bytes explicit per record) |
| server_write_IV | 4 |
| **Total** | **40 bytes** |

Since PRF produces 32 bytes per iteration, we need 2 iterations:

$$
\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{server\_random} \;\|\; \text{client\_random})
$$
$$
\text{key\_block} = A(1) \,\|\, A(2) = 64 \text{ bytes (use first 40)}
$$

**Step 2a:** Compute $A(1)$:
$$
A(0) = \text{"key expansion"} + \text{server\_random} \;\|\, \text{client\_random}
$$
$$
A(1) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{MS},\; m=A(0))
$$

**Step 2b:** Compute $A(2)$:
$$
A(2) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{MS},\; m=A(1))
$$

**Step 2c:** Concatenate:
$$
\text{key\_block} = A(1) \,\|\, A(2)
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3: Partition the Key Block

$$
\text{key\_block} = \underbrace{K_{c,\text{enc}}}_{0..15} \;\|\; \underbrace{K_{s,\text{enc}}}_{16..31} \;\|\; \underbrace{IV_c}_{32..35} \;\|\; \underbrace{IV_s}_{36..39}
$$

Where:
- $K_{c,\text{enc}}$ = client's 16-byte AES-128 key
- $K_{s,\text{enc}}$ = server's 16-byte AES-128 key
- $IV_c$ = client's 4-byte implicit IV (GCM salt)
- $IV_s$ = server's 4-byte implicit IV

### 3.1.5 Step 4: Compute the Finished Message (Client)

The Finished message proves that both parties derived the same master secret:

$$
\text{verify\_data}_{\text{client}} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"client finished"},\; \text{Hash}(\text{all handshake msgs}))
$$

The "all handshake msgs" is the **concatenation of every handshake message** exchanged so far, in order, **including the ClientHello and ServerHello but excluding the ChangeCipherSpec and Finished messages themselves**.

$$
\text{verify\_data}_{\text{client}} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"client finished"},\; \text{SHA-256}(\text{handshake\_log}))
$$

The result is exactly **12 bytes** for SHA-256.

**Server's Finished:**
$$
\text{verify\_data}_{\text{server}} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"server finished"},\; \text{SHA-256}(\text{handshake\_log} \,\|\, \text{client\_finished}))
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The server appends the client's Finished to its hash input — this prevents **man-in-the-middle replay** of Finished messages.

### 3.1.6 Step 5: Encrypting Application Data with AES-128-GCM

For each record, AES-GCM needs a 12-byte IV. The IV is constructed as:
$$
\text{IV}_{\text{record}} = IV_{\text{implicit} \,\|\, \text{explicit}} = \underbrace{IV_c}_{4 \text{ bytes (salt)}} \;\|\; \underbrace{\text{8-byte nonce from sender}_{8 \text{ bytes}}
$$

**Encryption step:**
$$
C = \text{AES-128-Encrypt}(K_{c,\text{enc}},\; \text{Plaintext} \oplus J_0 \oplus \text{ctr}_1 \;\|\; \text{ctr}_2 \;\|\; \cdots)
$$

Where $J_0$ is the pre-counter block and $\text{ctr}_i$ is the incremented counter.

**Authentication tag:**
$$
T = \text{GHASH}(H,\; \text{Associated\_Data},\; C) \oplus E_{K_{c,\text{enc}}}(J_0)
$$

The final record on the wire is:
$$
\text{TLS Record} = \underbrace{\text{Header}}_{\text{type + version + length}} \;\|\; \underbrace{C}_{\text{ciphertext}} \;\|\; \underbrace{T}_{16 \text{ byte tag}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Vulnerability & Validation Map

This matrix maps **common TLS attacks** to the **testing tool** that detects them — a **must-know** for the KTU 2024 lab exam viva.

| Attack | Description | CWE / CVE | Detection Tool | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **POODLE** | Padding Oracle on Downgraded Legacy Encryption (SSLv3 CBC) | CVE-2014-3566 | `testssl.sh --poodle` | Disable SSLv3 |
| **BEAST** | Browser Exploit Against SSL/TLS (TLS 1.0 CBC) | CVE-2011-3389 | `nmap --script ssl-beast` | Use TLS 1.2+ with AEAD |
| **Heartbleed** | OpenSSL heartbeat buffer over-read | CVE-2014-0160 | `nmap -sV --script=ssl-heartbleed` | Patch OpenSSL ≥ 1.0.1g |
| **CRIME** | Compression Ratio Info-leak Made Easy | CVE-2012-4929 | `testssl.sh --crime` | Disable TLS compression |
| **BREACH** | Browser Reconnaissance and Exfiltration via Adaptive Compression of Hypertext | CVE-2013-3587 | Manual | Disable HTTP compression |
| **Sweet32** | Birthday attack on 64-bit block ciphers (3DES, Blowfish) | CVE-2016-2183 | `nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers` | Disable 3DES |
| **ROBOT** | Return Of Bleichenbacher's Oracle Threat (RSA PKCS#1 v1.5) | CVE-2017-13099 | `testssl.sh --robot` | Use RSA-PSS or ECDHE |
| **DROWN** | Decrypting RSA with Obsolete and Weakened eNcryption (SSLv2) | CVE-2016-0800 | `nmap --script ssl-drown` | Disable SSLv2 |
| **Logjam** | Weak DH parameters (512-bit export) | CVE-2015-4000 | `testssl.sh --logjam` | Use ≥ 2048-bit DH |
| **Invalid Cert** | Self-signed / expired / untrusted CA | N/A | `openssl s_client -verify` | Install valid CA-signed cert |
| **Weak Cipher** | RC4, 3DES, NULL, EXPORT, DES | N/A | `nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers` | Restrict to AES-GCM/ChaCha20 |
| **TLS Version Downgrade** | Server accepts SSLv3/TLS 1.0 | N/A | `openssl s_client -ssl3` | Set `MinProtocol = TLSv1.2` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Viva Hit:** A student must know how to **enumerate cipher suites, identify weak ciphers, validate certificate chains, and verify TLS version restrictions** on a target server. Memorize at least **5 tools** and **3 attacks** from this matrix.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: X.509 Certificate Chain Validation

A **digital certificate** is essentially a signed assertion by a **Certificate Authority (CA)** that a public key belongs to a given identity.

### 3.3.1 Certificate Structure (X.509 v3)

$$
\text{Cert} = \{\text{TBSCertificate},\; \text{SignatureAlgorithm},\; \text{SignatureValue}\}
$$

Where `TBSCertificate` (To-Be-Signed) contains:
- **Version:** `v3` (value 2)
- **Serial Number:** Unique CA-assigned ID
- **Signature Algorithm:** e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption`
- **Issuer:** `CN = DigiCert Global Root CA, O = DigiCert Inc`
- **Validity:** `Not Before`, `Not After`
- **Subject:** `CN = www.ktu.ac.in`
- **Subject Public Key Info:** `{Algorithm: RSA, Key: 2048-bit modulus}`
- **Extensions:** `subjectAltName`, `keyUsage`, `extendedKeyUsage`, `basicConstraints`

### 3.3.2 Validation Algorithm (RFC 5280)

For a server certificate $C_0$ to be valid, the client must verify:

**Step 1: Parse the certificate chain** $\text{Chain} = \{C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_n\}$ where $C_n$ is the trusted root.

**Step 2: Verify each signature** in the chain:
$$
\text{Verify}_{PK_{C_{i+1}}}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{TBS}_{C_i})) = \text{True}
$$

That is, the CA's public key must successfully verify the certificate's TBS signature.

**Step 3: Check validity dates:**
$$
\text{current\_time} \in [\text{NotBefore},\; \text{NotAfter}]
$$

**Step 4: Check revocation status:**
- **CRL (Certificate Revocation List):** Fetch the CA's CRL and confirm the cert's serial is not present.
- **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** Query the OCSP responder and check the response is "good".

**Step 5: Check key usage extensions:**
- Server cert: `keyUsage = digitalSignature, keyEncipherment`
- `extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth`

**Step 6: Check hostname match:**
- `subjectAltName` (SAN) must contain the hostname (`www.example.com`)
- Or `commonName` (CN) as a fallback

> **Conversion Logic:** If **any** of the above checks fail, the TLS handshake is aborted with a `bad_certificate` or `certificate_unknown` alert, and the user sees a browser warning.

### 3.3.3 Worked Example: Validating `https://www.ktu.ac.in`

Assume the chain is:
- `C_0` = `www.ktu.ac.in` (leaf, signed by `C_1`)
- `C_1` = `DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1` (intermediate, signed by `C_2`)
- `C_2` = `DigiCert Global Root CA` (trusted root in browser store)

**Verification matrix:**

| Check | Operation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Signature of $C_0$ | `Verify` with `PK_{C_1}` | ✅ Valid (RSA-2048) |
| Signature of $C_1$ | `Verify` with `PK_{C_2}` | ✅ Valid (RSA-2048) |
| $C_0$ not expired | `current_time ∈ [2024-01-01, 2025-12-31]` | ✅ |
| $C_0$ not revoked | OCSP check returns `good` | ✅ |
| $C_0$ key usage | `digitalSignature, keyEncipherment` | ✅ |
| Hostname match | `SAN: DNS:www.ktu.ac.in` | ✅ |

All checks pass → TLS connection proceeds with `$PK_{C_0}$ as the server's public key.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TLS 1.2 Full Handshake — Mermaid Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant clientA as Client (Browser)
    participant serverB as Server (ktu.ac.in)

    clientA->>serverB: ClientHello<br/>(version, client_random, cipher_suites, extensions)
    serverB-->>clientA: ServerHello<br/>(version, server_random, chosen_cipher)
    serverB-->>clientA: Certificate<br/>(X.509 chain)
    serverB-->>clientA: ServerKeyExchange<br/>(ECDHE params + signature)
    serverB-->>clientA: ServerHelloDone

    clientA->>serverB: ClientKeyExchange<br/>(ECDHE public value)
    clientA->>serverB: ChangeCipherSpec
    clientA->>serverB: Finished<br/>(verify_data_client)

    serverB->>clientA: ChangeCipherSpec
    serverB->>clientA: Finished<br/>(verify_data_server)

    Note over clientA,serverB: SECURE CHANNEL ESTABLISHED
    clientA->>serverB: Application Data (HTTP over TLS)
    serverB-->>clientA: Encrypted HTTP Response
```

## 4.2 TLS Record Protocol Pipeline — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appA[Application Data<br/>HTTP Request] --> fragA[Fragmentation<br/>max 16 KB per record]
    fragA --> compA[Compression<br/>optional - usually NULL]
    compA --> macA[MAC Computation<br/>HMAC-SHA256]
    macA --> encA[Encryption<br/>AES-128-GCM]
    encA --> hdrA[Add TLS Record Header<br/>type + version + length]
    hdrA --> tcpA[Send via TCP<br/>port 443]

    tcpA --> rhdrB[Receive TLS Record Header]
    rhdrB --> decB[Decrypt<br/>AES-128-GCM]
    decB --> vmacB[Verify HMAC Tag<br/>GHASH]
    vmacB --> decompB[Decompress<br/>if not NULL]
    decompB --> reassB[Reassemble Fragments]
    reassB --> appB[Deliver Plaintext<br/>to Application]

    style appA fill:#e1f5ff
    style appB fill:#e1f5ff
    style encA fill:#ffe1e1
    style decB fill:#ffe1e1
    style macA fill:#fff4e1
    style vmacB fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.3 Certificate Chain-of-Trust Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootCA[Root CA<br/>DigiCert Global Root CA<br/>Self-Signed Trusted]
    intCA[Intermediate CA<br/>DigiCert TLS RSA SHA256 2020 CA1<br/>Signed by Root]
    serverCert[Server Certificate<br/>www.ktu.ac.in<br/>Signed by Intermediate]
    clientA[Client Browser<br/>Verifies Chain]

    rootCA -->|signs| intCA
    intCA -->|signs| serverCert
    serverCert -.->|presents to| clientA
    clientA -.->|checks| rootCA

    style rootCA fill:#90EE90
    style intCA fill:#FFD700
    style serverCert fill:#87CEEB
    style clientA fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.4 TLS Testing & Validation Workflow (Lab Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Lab<br/>Target: example.com] --> step1[Step 1: Identify Open Port 443<br/>nmap -sV -p 443 example.com]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Enumerate TLS Versions<br/>nmap --script ssl-enum-versions]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Enumerate Cipher Suites<br/>nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Inspect Certificate<br/>openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -showcerts]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Verify Certificate Chain<br/>check issuer, validity, SAN, OCSP]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Run testssl.sh<br/>testssl.sh example.com]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Test Specific Vulnerabilities<br/>Heartbleed, POODLE, ROBOT, Sweet32]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Capture & Analyze Handshake<br/>Wireshark tcp.port==443]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Document Findings<br/>Mark weak ciphers and old versions]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Apply Hardening<br/>Disable weak protocols and ciphers]
    step10 --> endA[End: Generate Lab Report]

    style startA fill:#90EE90
    style endA fill:#FFB6C1
    style step6 fill:#FFD700
    style step8 fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.5 TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3 Handshake Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant cA as Client
    participant sB as Server (TLS 1.3)

    Note over cA,sB: TLS 1.3 - 1-RTT Handshake
    cA->>sB: ClientHello<br/>(key_share, supported_versions, cipher_suites)
    sB-->>cA: ServerHello<br/>(key_share, selected_version)
    sB-->>cA: EncryptedExtensions
    sB-->>cA: Certificate
    sB-->>cA: CertificateVerify
    sB-->>cA: Finished

    cA->>sB: Finished<br/>+ Application Data (0-RTT possible)

    Note over cA,sB: TLS 1.2 needed 2 RTTs; TLS 1.3 needs 1 RTT
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between SSL and TLS. List any four major improvements introduced in TLS 1.3 over TLS 1.2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SSL (v3.0) | TLS (1.0–1.2) | TLS 1.3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | Netscape proprietary | IETF RFC 2246, 4346, 5246 | RFC 8446 (2018) |
| PRF | MD5 + SHA-1 | HMAC-SHA256/384 | HKDF (HMAC-based KDF) |
| Handshake | 2 RTT | 2 RTT | **1 RTT** (0-RTT resumption) |
| Cipher Suites | Many (incl. EXPORT, NULL) | AES-CBC, AES-GCM | **Only AEAD**: AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 |
| Key Exchange | RSA-only, FIXED-RSA | RSA, DHE, ECDHE | **Only (EC)DHE** (no static RSA) |
| Compression | Supported | Supported (deprecated) | **Removed** (CRIME attack) |
| MAC-then-Encrypt | Yes (CBC) | Yes (CBC) | **Encrypt-then-MAC (AEAD)** |
| Session Resumption | Session ID only | Session ID + Tickets | **PSK + 0-RTT** |

**Four key improvements in TLS 1.3:**
1. **Mandatory forward secrecy** — RSA key transport removed; only (EC)DHE allowed.
2. **Faster handshake** — 1-RTT mandatory, 0-RTT resumption.
3. **AEAD-only encryption** — CBC suites eliminated (no more Lucky 13, BEAST).
4. **Encrypted SNI / Encrypted Extensions** — privacy against passive eavesdroppers.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a cipher suite? Explain the meaning of `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256` with respect to its key exchange, authentication, encryption, and MAC algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cipher suite** is a named combination of four cryptographic algorithms negotiated during the TLS handshake: **key exchange, authentication, bulk encryption, and MAC (or AEAD)**.

For `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`:

| Component | Algorithm | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Exchange** | **ECDHE** | Ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman — provides **forward secrecy** (new key per session) |
| **Authentication** | **RSA** | Server's identity proven via RSA-signed certificate |
| **Bulk Encryption** | **AES-128-GCM** | 128-bit AES in Galois/Counter Mode — AEAD cipher |
| **MAC / PRF** | **SHA-256** | SHA-256 used for PRF and authentication tag (GCM internal) |

**Why ECDHE over plain RSA?** ECDHE generates a fresh DH key pair per session, so even if the server's long-term RSA private key is later compromised, **past sessions remain secure** — this is **forward secrecy**.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCITL607 Network Security Lab]**

**(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the **TLS 1.2 handshake** in detail. List the cryptographic operations performed at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **process of testing and validating TLS/SSL configurations** on a web server using `nmap`, `OpenSSL`, and `testssl.sh`. Include sample commands. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — TLS 1.2 Handshake

**Step 1: ClientHello** (Client → Server)
- Carries: `client_version`, `client_random` (32 bytes), `session_id`, `cipher_suites` list, `compression_methods`, extensions.
- **Crypto operation:** None — pure negotiation.

**Step 2: ServerHello** (Server → Client)
- Carries: chosen `server_version`, `server_random`, `session_id`, `cipher_suite`, `compression_method`.
- **Crypto operation:** Server selects the strongest mutually supported suite.

**Step 3: Certificate** (Server → Client)
- Sends X.509 certificate chain: `[Leaf cert → Intermediate CA → Root CA]`.
- **Crypto operation:** RSA public key embedded in cert will be used for key transport (in static RSA) or DH params (in ECDHE).

**Step 4: ServerKeyExchange** (Server → Client) — *only for DHE/ECDHE*
- Contains DH/ECDH public value and **server's RSA signature** over `(client_random ‖ server_random ‖ DH_params)`.
- **Crypto operation:** `signature = RSA_sign(SHA-256(params))` — proves server owns the private key.

**Step 5: ServerHelloDone** — Marker, no payload.

**Step 6: ClientKeyExchange** (Client → Server)
- For RSA: PMS encrypted with server's public key: `E_PubK(PMS)`.
- For ECDHE: client's ECDH public value.
- **Crypto operation:** Establishes the **pre-master secret**.

**Step 7: CertificateVerify** (Client → Server) — *only for mutual TLS (mTLS)*
- Client signs hash of all handshake messages with its private key.
- **Crypto operation:** Proves client owns the certificate's private key.

**Step 8: ChangeCipherSpec** (Both directions)
- Single-byte signal: "all subsequent messages use new keys."

**Step 9: Finished** (Both directions)
- `verify_data = PRF(MS, "client/server finished", SHA-256(handshake_log))`
- **Crypto operation:** Authenticates the entire handshake transcript.

**Step 10: Application Data** — Encrypted with derived session keys.

**Cryptographic Summary Table:**

| Step | Message | Cryptographic Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ClientHello | None |
| 2 | ServerHello | Server's suite selection |
| 3 | Certificate | RSA/EC public key delivery |
| 4 | ServerKeyExchange | RSA-sign(DH params) |
| 6 | ClientKeyExchange | `E_PubK(PMS)` or ECDH value |
| 9 | Finished | `PRF(MS, label, SHA256(messages))` |
| 10 | App Data | AES-GCM with derived keys |

**Diagram reference:** See **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### Solution (b) — Testing & Validation Procedure

**Step 1: Identify TLS Service (Port Discovery)**

```bash
nmap -sV -p 443,993,995,465 example.com
```

- `-sV`: Probe open ports to determine service/version.
- Output: `443/tcp open ssl/http Apache httpd 2.4.41`

**Step 2: Enumerate Supported TLS Versions**

```bash
nmap --script ssl-enum-versions -p 443 example.com
```

- Checks for SSLv3, TLS 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3.
- **Expected:** Only TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 should be enabled.

**Step 3: Enumerate Supported Cipher Suites**

```bash
nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 example.com
```

- Output lists each cipher (e.g., `AES_128_GCM_SHA256`) as "A" (acceptable) or "C" (weak).
- **Mark weak ciphers** (RC4, 3DES, NULL, EXPORT) as critical findings.

**Step 4: Inspect the Server Certificate**

```bash
openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -showcerts </dev/null
```

- Display: subject, issuer, validity dates, public key, signature algorithm.
- Pipe `</dev/null` to terminate the connection after handshake.

**Step 5: Verify Certificate Chain**

```bash
openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -verify 5
```

- `-verify 5`: Verify up to 5 levels of CA chain.
- Output: `Verify return code: 0 (ok)` is good; non-zero indicates problems.

**Step 6: Check Certificate Validity Dates**

```bash
echo | openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
```

- Output: `notBefore=Jan 1 00:00:00 2024 GMT`, `notAfter=Dec 31 23:59:59 2025 GMT`.

**Step 7: Run Comprehensive Testssl.sh Scan**

```bash
testssl.sh --color 0 example.com
```

- Tests: protocol support, cipher suite strength, certificate details, vulnerabilities (Heartbleed, ROBOT, POODLE, Sweet32, etc.).
- Generates an HTML report: `https://testssl.sh` documentation.

**Step 8: Capture Live Handshake with Wireshark**

```bash
# Filter: tls.handshake.type == 1
```

- Wireshark display filter for handshake messages.
- Inspect: ClientHello (CipherSuites list), ServerHello (Chosen cipher), Certificate, etc.

**Step 9: Test Specific Known Vulnerabilities**

```bash
nmap -p 443 --script ssl-heartbleed,ssl-poodle,ssl-drown example.com
```

- Heartbleed: `ssl-heartbleed`
- POODLE: `ssl-poodle`
- DROWN: `ssl-drown`

**Step 10: Generate Lab Report**

Document the following:

| Test | Tool | Expected | Found | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TLS 1.0/1.1 disabled | nmap | Not present | ❌ | ❌ FAIL |
| TLS 1.2 enabled | nmap | Present | ✅ | ✅ PASS |
| TLS 1.3 enabled | nmap | Present | ✅ | ✅ PASS |
| No RC4/3DES/NULL | nmap enum-ciphers | Not present | ❌ | ❌ FAIL |
| Cert valid ≥ 30 days | openssl | ≥ 30 | 89 days | ✅ PASS |
| Cert chain trusted | openssl -verify | `Verify return code: 0` | `0` | ✅ PASS |
| OCSP staple | openssl | Present | ✅ | ✅ PASS |
| Heartbleed absent | nmap | Not vulnerable | Not vulnerable | ✅ PASS |

**(7 marks)** ✅

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PCITL607]**

**(a)** Explain the **key derivation process** in TLS 1.2, from the pre-master secret to the final session keys. Include the formulas and a numerical illustration of the PRF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **X.509 certificate validation chain** in detail. How does a browser verify the trustworthiness of a server's certificate? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Key Derivation in TLS 1.2

**Step 1: Pre-Master Secret (PMS)**
- For **RSA key transport:** Client generates a 48-byte random PMS, encrypts with server's public RSA key, sends via `ClientKeyExchange`.
- For **ECDHE:** Both parties exchange ephemeral ECDH public values and compute:
$$
\text{PMS} = d_{\text{client}} \cdot Q_{\text{server}} = d_{\text{server}} \cdot Q_{\text{client}}
$$
This is a 32-byte value for P-256.

**Step 2: Master Secret (MS)**

$$
\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(\text{PMS},\; \text{"master secret"},\; \text{client\_random} \;\|\; \text{server\_random})
$$

**Step 3: Key Block Generation**

$$
\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{MS},\; \text{"key expansion"},\; \text{server\_random} \;\|\; \text{client\_random})
$$

**Step 4: Partition the Key Block**

For `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`:
- `client_write_key` = first 16 bytes
- `server_write_key` = next 16 bytes
- `client_write_IV` = next 4 bytes (GCM salt)
- `server_write_IV` = next 4 bytes

**Step 5: PRF Internal Construction (Numerical Illustration)**

Assume:
- `PMS` = 32 bytes of zeros (for illustration): `00 00 00 ... 00`
- `client_random` = `AA AA AA ... AA` (32 bytes)
- `server_random` = `BB BB BB ... BB` (32 bytes)
- PRF uses SHA-256.

The label `"master secret"` = 13 ASCII bytes: `6D 61 73 74 65 72 20 73 65 63 72 65 74`

**Compute $A(0)$:**
$$
A(0) = \text{label} \,\|\, \text{client\_random} \,\|\, \text{server\_random}
$$
$$
A(0) = \text{"master secret"} \;\|\; \text{0xAA} \times 32 \;\|\; \text{0xBB} \times 32
$$
Length = 13 + 32 + 32 = 77 bytes.

**Compute $A(1)$:**
$$
A(1) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{PMS},\; m=A(0))
$$

This yields a 32-byte hash. For real keys, this would be a deterministic 256-bit value.

**Compute $A(2)$:**
$$
A(2) = \text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA-256}}(K=\text{PMS},\; m=A(1))
$$

**Concatenate and truncate to 48 bytes:**
$$
\text{MS} = (A(1) \,\|\, A(2))[0:48]
$$

**Key Block Derivation:**

Repeat the PRF with `"key expansion"` label and `server_random ‖ client_random`:
$$
\text{key\_block} = A(1) \,\|\, A(2) = 64 \text{ bytes (truncate to 40)}
$$

**Final Key Allocation:**

| Segment | Bytes | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 0–15 | `client_write_key` | AES-128 key for client→server |
| 16–31 | `server_write_key` | AES-128 key for server→client |
| 32–35 | `client_write_IV` | 4-byte GCM salt (client) |
| 36–39 | `server_write_IV` | 4-byte GCM salt (server) |

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### Solution (b) — X.509 Certificate Validation Chain

A **digital certificate** is a digitally signed electronic document that binds a **public key** to an **identity** (domain, organization, individual). **X.509 v3** is the standard format used in TLS.

### Certificate Hierarchy

```
Root CA (self-signed, in OS/browser trust store)
    │
    ├── Intermediate CA (signed by Root)
    │       │
    │       └── Server Certificate (signed by Intermediate)
    │                  │
    │                  └── Subject: CN=www.example.com
```

### Validation Steps (RFC 5280)

**Step 1: Chain Construction**

The server sends its certificate chain. The client builds a path:
$$
\text{Chain} = \{C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_n\}
$$
Where $C_0$ is the leaf (server) certificate and $C_n$ is the trust anchor (root CA).

**Step 2: Signature Verification**

For each certificate in the chain (except the root), verify the signature using the **issuer's public key**:
$$
\text{Verify}_{PK_{C_{i+1}}}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{TBS}_{C_i})) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{True}
$$

**Step 3: Validity Period Check**
$$
\text{NotBefore} \leq \text{current\_time} \leq \text{NotAfter}
$$
If outside the window → reject with `certificate_expired`.

**Step 4: Revocation Check**

Two methods:

**(a) CRL (Certificate Revocation List):**
- Download `http://crl.digicert.com/...crl`
- Search for cert's serial number.
- If present → cert is **revoked**.

**(b) OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):**
- POST to `http://ocsp.digicert.com` with cert serial.
- Response: `good`, `revoked`, or `unknown`.

**Step 5: Key Usage & Extended Key Usage**
- Server cert: `keyUsage = digitalSignature, keyEncipherment`
- `extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth` (or `id-kp-1`)

**Step 6: Hostname Verification**
- Compare the requested hostname (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`) against the `subjectAltName (SAN)` extension.
- Wildcard match allowed: `*.example.com` matches `www.example.com`.

**Step 7: Path Constraints**
- Each CA cert must have `basicConstraints = CA:TRUE`.
- `pathLenConstraint` limits depth of subordinate CAs.

**Step 8: Algorithm Strength**
- Reject SHA-1 signatures (deprecated).
- Require RSA ≥ 2048 bits or ECC ≥ 256 bits.
- Reject certificates using `md5WithRSAEncryption` or `sha1WithRSAEncryption`.

### Browser-Side Actions on Failure

| Failure | Browser Action |
|---|---|
| Expired | Red padlock, "Your connection is not private" |
| Self-signed | Red padlock, "NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID" |
| Hostname mismatch | Red padlock, "NET::ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID" |
| Revoked (OCSP) | Red padlock, "NET::ERR_CERT_REVOKED" |
| Untrusted root | Red padlock, "NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID" |
| Weak signature | Modern browsers block the connection silently |

**(7 marks)** ✅

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in TLS-related questions (losing 2-4 marks per question):**
> 1. **Confusing PMS and MS** — Pre-Master Secret is 48 bytes (or DH result); Master Secret is the 48-byte PRF output. They are **not** the same.
> 2. **Skipping the certificate validation steps** — Examiners expect **at least 5 steps** (signature, validity, revocation, key usage, hostname). Listing only 2 will lose marks.
> 3. **Forgetting `openssl s_client` flags** — `-showcerts`, `-verify`, `-tls1_2`, `-cipher` are commonly asked. Memorize them.
> 4. **Writing cipher suite components in wrong order** — Format is always `KeyExchange_Auth_WITH_Enc_MAC` (e.g., `ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`).
> 5. **Not mentioning AEAD vs MAC-then-Encrypt** — Modern AEAD suites (GCM, ChaCha20) combine encryption and integrity, unlike CBC suites which need separate MAC.
> 6. **Omitting forward secrecy explanation** — ECDHE/DHE provide forward secrecy; static RSA does NOT. Examiners love this distinction.
> 7. **Forgetting SNI (Server Name Indication)** — A TLS extension for virtual hosting; required for HTTPS on shared IPs.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCITL607 Module 2 Exam:**

- [ ] TLS provides **confidentiality, integrity, authentication** for web traffic.
- [ ] TLS 1.2 uses **2 RTT** handshake; TLS 1.3 uses **1 RTT** (or 0-RTT for resumption).
- [ ] Cipher suite format: `KeyExchange_Auth_WITH_Enc_MAC` (e.g., `ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`).
- [ ] **PRF** in TLS 1.2 is `P_SHA256(secret, label + seed)`.
- [ ] **PMS** is the raw shared secret; **MS** is `PRF(PMS, "master secret", C_rand ‖ S_rand)`.
- [ ] **Key block** is `PRF(MS, "key expansion", S_rand ‖ C_rand)`; partitioned into 4–6 keys.
- [ ] **AEAD ciphers** (GCM, ChaCha20) are mandatory in TLS 1.3.
- [ ] **Forward secrecy** requires ephemeral DH (DHE or ECDHE) — NOT static RSA.
- [ ] **X.509 chain validation**: signature, validity, revocation (OCSP/CRL), key usage, hostname (SAN).
- [ ] **Tools for testing**: `nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers`, `openssl s_client -showcerts`, `testssl.sh`, Wireshark.
- [ ] **Critical vulnerabilities**: POODLE (SSLv3), Heartbleed (OpenSSL ≤ 1.0.1f), BEAST (TLS 1.0 CBC), ROBOT (RSA PKCS#1 v1.5), Sweet32 (3DES), Logjam (weak DH).
- [ ] **Hardening checklist**: disable SSLv2/SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1; restrict to AEAD ciphers; use ≥ 2048-bit RSA or ≥ 256-bit ECC; enable OCSP stapling; use HSTS.
- [ ] **TLS 1.3 improvements**: 1-RTT handshake, mandatory AEAD, encrypted SNI, 0-RTT resumption, no static RSA.
- [ ] **Default port**: TCP 443 for HTTPS; 993 (IMAPS), 995 (POP3S), 465 (SMTPS), 587 (SMTP+STARTTLS).
- [ ] **Record protocol pipeline**: Fragment → Compress → MAC → Encrypt → Add Header.
- [ ] **Finished message** = `PRF(MS, "client/server finished", SHA-256(handshake_log))` — authenticates handshake.
